question: Find the complete set of solutions to $-8<6-\dfrac{x}{2}$.

steps.title: Clear the fraction
steps.content:
Multiply through by $-2$. Reverse the inequality because the multiplier is negative:

$$28 > x$$

solution.fast: Move constants, then multiply by $-2$ and reverse: $x < 28$.
solution.trap: $x < 22$ comes from forgetting to reverse when multiplying by a negative.

question: A nuclide $^{214}_{82}\text{Pb}$ changes by radioactive decay into the nuclide $^{210}_{82}\text{Pb}$. Which combination of emissions produces this change?

steps.title: Mass number drops by so exactly alpha particle
steps.content: Mass number drops by 4 (214→210), so exactly 1 alpha particle is emitted (mass -4, charge -2).

steps.title: This alone would drop atomic number by but
steps.content: This alone would drop atomic number by 2 (82→80), but atomic number stays at 82, so 2 beta emissions are needed to raise Z back by 2 (each beta: Z+1, mass unchanged).

question: Which one of the following is a simplification of $(\sqrt3-\sqrt2)^2$?

solution.trap: $5-\sqrt{2}\sqrt{3}$ drops the factor of 2 on the middle term.

question: The graph shown of quantity y against quantity x represents the motion of a body. [DIAGRAM: straight line through origin passing through point (2.0, 10), axes in SI units, g = 10 N/kg]. Which two of the following could the graph represent? (1) kinetic energy against velocity for an object of mass 10 kg undergoing free-fall; (2) potential energy against height for an object of mass 20 kg being lifted by a constant external force; (3) velocity against time for an object of mass 20 kg being accelerated by a resultant force of 100 N; (4) work done by an external force of 5 N against distance moved for an object of mass 12 kg being moved at constant speed by (and in the direction of) the external force.

steps.title: The graph is a straight line through the
steps.content: The graph is a straight line through the origin with gradient $10/2.0 = 5$.

steps.title: Option so a straight line of gradient fits
steps.content: Option 3: $a = F/m = 100/20 = 5\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, so $v = 5t$, a straight line of gradient 5 — fits.

steps.title: Option is quadratic not linear doesn t fit
steps.content: Option 1: $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = 5v^2$ is quadratic, not linear — doesn't fit.

question: The ratio of $Q:R$ is $5:2$ and the ratio of $R:S$ is $3:10$. Which one of the following gives the ratio $Q:S$ in its simplest form?

A uranium-235 nucleus can undergo fission to produce two smaller nuclei. [DIAGRAM: three candidate fission reaction diagrams]. Diagram 1: a proton plus $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$ produces $^{89}_{36}\text{Kr}$, $^{144}_{56}\text{Ba}$, and 2 neutrons. Diagram 2: a neutron plus $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$ produces $^{96}_{38}\text{Sr}$, $^{137}_{54}\text{Xe}$, and 3 neutrons. Diagram 3: a neutron plus $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$ produces $^{145}_{57}\text{La}$, $^{87}_{35}\text{Br}$, and 3 neutrons. Which of the diagrams, if any, could represent this process?

steps.title: Diagram is invalid because it shows a proton
steps.content: Diagram 1 is invalid because it shows a proton bombarding the uranium nucleus rather than a neutron, which does not represent standard fission.

steps.title: Diagram check conservation mass numbers atomic numbers valid
steps.content: Diagram 2: check conservation — mass numbers $235+1 = 96+137+3 = 236$ ✓, atomic numbers $92 = 38+54 = 92$ ✓, valid.

steps.title: Diagram mass numbers but products give does not
steps.content: Diagram 3: mass numbers $235+1=236$ but products give $145+87+3=235$ — does not balance, invalid.

question: The mean age of the twenty members of a running club is exactly 28. The mean age increases by exactly 2 years when two new members join. What is the mean age of the two new members?

A circuit consists of a 5.0 Ω resistor and a variable resistor connected in series with a 24 V battery. The variable resistor has a minimum resistance of 3.0 Ω and a maximum resistance of 15 Ω. The battery and the connecting wires have negligible resistance. What is the maximum power dissipated in the 5.0 Ω resistor?

steps.title: Maximum power in the resistor occurs at maximum
steps.content: Maximum power in the 5.0 Ω resistor occurs at maximum current, i.e., minimum total resistance, i.e., variable resistor at 3.0 Ω.

question: A medical scanner is bought for £15 000. The value of the scanner depreciates by 20% every year. By how much has the scanner reduced in value after 2 years?

solution.trap: $\pounds 6000$ is two separate 20% cuts of the original price, not compound depreciation.

question: The total power $P$ radiated by a star is given by $P = kR^2T^4$ where $R$ is the radius of the star, $T$ is its surface temperature and $k$ is a constant. The power currently radiated by the Sun is $4.0\times10^{26}$ W. Towards the end of the Sun's life its radius will increase by a factor of a hundred and its surface temperature will decrease by a factor of two. What will be the power radiated by the Sun when these changes have occurred?

steps.title: New power k R T kR T kR

question: The point A is 4 km due East of the point B. The bearing of the point C from A is $330°$ and the bearing of C from B is $060°$. Find the distance BC.

steps.title: Find angle at B
steps.content: From B, BA is due West and BC has bearing $060^\circ$, so $\angle ABC = 90^\circ-60^\circ=30^\circ$.

steps.title: Find angle at A
steps.content: From A, AB is due West and AC has bearing $330^\circ$, so $\angle BAC = 330^\circ-270^\circ=60^\circ$.

question: A transverse wave travelling through a medium has a frequency of 5.0 Hz, a wavelength of 4.0 cm and an amplitude of 3.0 cm. What is the total distance travelled by a particle of the medium in one minute?

steps.title: In one full oscillation a particle travels a
steps.content: In one full oscillation, a particle travels a distance of $4\times$ amplitude $= 4\times3.0=12$ cm.

steps.title: In one second there are oscillations so distance
steps.content: In one second there are 5 oscillations, so distance per second $= 5\times12=60$ cm.

question: The quantities $x$ and $y$ are positive. $x$ is inversely proportional to the square root of $y$. When $x=8$, $y=9$. What is the value of $y$ when $x=6$?

A motor is used to lift a mass of 5.0 kg using a pulley system. [DIAGRAM: a fixed pulley attached to the roof by a coupling; a rope passes over the pulley with the mass on one side and the motor pulling the other side]. The motor needs to cause the mass to accelerate upwards at 0.80 m s$^{-2}$. What is the minimum tension force that the coupling must be able to withstand without breaking? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; frictionless, massless pulley; massless, inextensible rope.)

steps.title: Tension in the rope supporting the mass N
steps.content: Tension in the rope supporting the mass: $T = m(g+a) = 5.0\times(10+0.80)=54$ N.

steps.title: Since the rope passes over a single fixed
steps.content: Since the rope passes over a single fixed pulley with the same tension throughout, and the coupling supports both sides of the rope pulling down on the pulley, the coupling must withstand $2T = 2\times54=108$ N.

question: In a trapezium PQRS, the parallel sides are PQ and RS. $PQ=(x-1)$ cm, $RS=(x+5)$ cm and the vertical height $QR=x$ cm. The area of the trapezium is 120 cm². What is the length of RS?

subtopic: charge, energy, voltage in series circuit
question: A heater is connected in series with a resistor and a 6.0 V battery. The total resistance of the circuit is 15 Ω. In 3.0 minutes, 180 J of electrical energy is transferred into other forms in the heater. How much charge flows through the heater in the 3.0 minutes and what is the voltage across the heater?

steps.title: Power in heater W so
steps.content: Power in heater $= 180\text{ J}/180\text{ s}=1.0$ W $=one^2R_{heater}$, so $R_{heater}=1.0/0.40^2=6.25\,\Omega$.

question: Make $b$ the subject of the formula: $a=\dfrac{b^2+2}{3b^2-1}$

solution.trap: The form with $3a+1$ in the denominator comes from a sign error when rearranging.

question: A cubic block has a hole through it with a square cross-section. [DIAGRAM: cube of side 10 cm with a 5.0 cm square hole through it]. The weight of the block is 30 N. What is the density of the material from which the block is made? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$.)

steps.title: Volume of square cross section hole cm cm
steps.content: Volume of square-cross-section hole (5 cm \times 5 cm, through 10 cm) $=5\times5\times10=250\text{ cm}^3$.

subtopic: volume of cylinder from rolled sheet
question: A thin rectangular sheet of metal 10 m by 5 m is made into an open ended cylinder by joining the edges PS and QR. The height of the cylinder is 10 m. What is the volume, in cubic metres, enclosed by this cylinder?

steps.title: Circumference from the sheet
steps.content: Height is 10 m, so the 5 m side is the circumference: $$2\pi r=5 \Rightarrow r=\dfrac{5}{2\pi}$$.

question: The diagram shows four solid steel balls P, Q, R and S of identical size. Balls P and R have shiny surfaces; balls Q and S have dull surfaces. Balls P and Q are in a room at 20 °C, both currently at 30 °C. Balls R and S are in a room at 40 °C, both currently at 40 °C. Which two balls lose thermal energy by convection, and which ball emits thermal radiation at the greatest rate?

steps.title: Balls P and Q at C are hotter
steps.content: Balls P and Q (at 30 ^\circ\mathrm{C}) are hotter than their surrounding room air (20 ^\circ\mathrm{C}), so they lose heat by convection to the surrounding air.

steps.title: Balls R and S are at the same
steps.content: Balls R and S are at the same temperature as their room (40 ^\circ\mathrm{C}), so there is no net convective loss.

steps.title: For radiation dull matte surfaces are better emitters
steps.content: For radiation, dull (matte) surfaces are better emitters than shiny surfaces at a given temperature; among P (shiny, 30^\circ\mathrm{C}), Q (dull, 30^\circ\mathrm{C}), R (shiny, 40^\circ\mathrm{C}), S (dull, 40^\circ\mathrm{C}), the highest temperature combined with a dull surface gives the greatest radiation rate: ball S.

[DIAGRAM: velocity-time graph, velocity axis from -2.0 to 8.0 m/s, time axis 0 to 30 s in segments of 10 s]. What total distance did the object travel in the 30 s, how far from its starting position was it at the end of the 30 s, and what was its average speed over the 30 s?

steps.title: From the v t graph velocity is at
steps.content: From the v-t graph: velocity is $8.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ at $t=0$, decreasing linearly to $0$ at $t=20\text{s}$, then continuing to $-2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ at $t=30\text{s}$.

steps.title: Displacement net distance from start m

question: During summer activities week 120 students each chose one activity from swimming, archery, and tennis. 46 of the students were girls (so 74 were boys). 36 of the students chose tennis, and $\tfrac{2}{3}$ of these were boys; 25 girls chose swimming, and 27 students chose archery. A boy is picked at random. What is the probability that he chose swimming?

steps.title: Fill tennis and girls
steps.content: Boys in tennis $=\tfrac23\times 36=24$, girls in tennis $=12$. Girls in archery $=46-12-25=9$.

steps.title: Boys in swimming
steps.content: Archery total 27 $\Rightarrow$ boys in archery $=18$. Boys total 74, so boys swimming $=74-24-18=32$.

question: Bronze is a mixture of tin and copper. A particular sample of bronze contains 10% tin by volume (10% of the total volume is tin, 90% is copper). What percentage of the mass of the sample is tin? (Density of tin $=Y$ and density of copper $=X$.)

steps.title: Masses from unit volume
steps.content: Volume 1: tin mass $0.1Y$, copper mass $0.9X$, total mass $0.1Y+0.9X$.

question: Which one of the following expressions is equivalent to $\dfrac{9^{2n+1}\times3^{4-3n}}{27^{2-n}}$?

When a stationary uranium-238 nucleus decays by alpha emission it forms a nucleus of thorium-234. The total kinetic energy produced by the decay is $E$. What is the kinetic energy of the alpha particle?

steps.title: By conservation of momentum the thorium nucleus and
steps.content: By conservation of momentum, the thorium nucleus and alpha particle recoil with equal and opposite momenta.

steps.title: Kinetic energy so KE is shared inversely proportional
steps.content: Kinetic energy $=p^2/2m$, so KE is shared inversely proportional to mass: $KE_\alpha = E\times\dfrac{m_{Th}}{m_{Th}+m_\alpha}=E\times\dfrac{234}{238}$.

question: In the diagram, PQRS is part of a regular polygon with $n$ sides. The side PQ is extended to T such that PQT is a straight line. The length of RQ is the same as the length of RT. Find an equation for $n$ in terms of $x$, where $x$ is the size of angle $\angle QRT$ in degrees.

steps.title: Exterior angle at Q
steps.content: Interior angle $\dfrac{(n-2)180}{n}$. Straight line at T gives $\angle RQT=\dfrac{360}{n}$.

steps.title: Isosceles triangle RQT
steps.content: $RQ=RT$, so base angles equal: $\angle RQT=\angle RTQ=\dfrac{360}{n}$.

question: A student carries out an experiment to measure the speed of sound. A loudspeaker that emits sound in all directions is placed between two buildings that are 128 m apart. The student and loudspeaker are 48 m from one of the buildings (and thus 80 m from the other). The loudspeaker emits regular clicks; each click results in two echoes, one from each building. The rate of clicks is gradually increased from zero until each echo coincides with a new click being emitted. What is the frequency of emission of clicks when this happens? (Speed of sound in air = 320 m s$^{-1}$.)

steps.title: Round trip time to nearer building s
steps.content: Round trip time to nearer building: $t_1=2\times48/320=0.3$ s.

steps.title: Round trip time to farther building s
steps.content: Round trip time to farther building: $t_2=2\times80/320=0.5$ s.

steps.title: For every echo to coincide with a click
steps.content: For every echo to coincide with a click, the click period must be a common factor of both 0.3 s and 0.5 s; the largest such period (giving highest, i.e. correct, frequency where this first happens) is $\gcd(0.3,0.5)=0.1$ s.

question: When $x=2$ is substituted in the expression $x^3+px^2+qx+p^2$ the result is 0. When $x=1$ is substituted into the same expression, the result is $-3.5$. Find all possible value(s) of $p$.

question: A parachutist is falling at terminal speed with his parachute open. Separate force diagrams show the vertical forces on the parachute and on the parachutist: air resistance on parachute (L), weight of parachute (M), force from parachutist on parachute (N), force from parachute on parachutist (P), air resistance on parachutist (Q), weight of parachutist (R). Consider: Equation 1: $L=M+N$; Equation 2: $R=P+Q$; Equation 3: $L=Q$; Equation 4: $N=P$; Equation 5: $L+R=Q+M$. Which of these equations, if any, is/are the direct result of applying Newton's Third Law to this situation?

steps.title: Newton s Third Law applies to the interaction
steps.content: Newton's Third Law applies to the interaction pair between the two contacting bodies (parachute and parachutist): the force from the parachute on the parachutist (P) and the force from the parachutist on the parachute (N) are equal and opposite in magnitude, giving $N=P$ (equation 4).

steps.title: Equations and follow from equilibrium Newton s first
steps.content: Equations 1, 2 and 5 follow from equilibrium (Newton's first/second law at terminal velocity, since each object is in equilibrium), not from the third law.

steps.title: Equation is not a required equality by any
steps.content: Equation 3 ($L=Q$) is not a required equality by any law.

subtopic: equation of a circle inscribed in a square
question: A square PQRS is drawn above the x-axis with the side PQ on the x-axis. P is the point $(-5,0)$ and Q is the point $(1,0)$. A circle is drawn inside the square with diameter equal in length to the side of the square. Which one of the following is an equation of the circle?

steps.title: Side length so the square has side with
steps.content: Side length $PQ = 1-(-5)=6$, so the square has side 6 with $S=(-5,6)$, $R=(1,6)$.

steps.title: The inscribed circle has diameter equal to the
steps.content: The inscribed circle has diameter equal to the side (6), so radius 3, centred at the square's centre $(-2,3)$.

The helicopter and crate are accelerating upwards at 2.0 m s$^{-2}$. What is the tension in the rope? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; air resistance can be ignored.)

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the

question: The first term of a convergent geometric series is 8. The fifth term is 2. The sixth term is real and positive. What is the sum to infinity of this series? (Sum to infinity $=\dfrac{a}{1-r}$.)

steps.title: The sixth term must be positive since this
steps.content: The sixth term $ar^5$ must be positive; since $a>0$, this requires $r^5>0$, i.e. $r>0$, so $r=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

question: A shopper pushes a supermarket trolley a distance of 15 m in a straight line across a level, horizontal surface. The shopper applies a constant force of 50 N at an angle of 37° below the horizontal. The total weight of the trolley and its contents is 350 N. What is the magnitude of the total vertical force that the surface exerts on the trolley and how much work is done by the pushing force? (sin 37° = 0.60; cos 37° = 0.80.)

steps.title: Vertical component of push downward since pushed below
steps.content: Vertical component of push (downward, since pushed below horizontal) $=50\sin37^\circ=50\times0.60=30$ N.

steps.title: Work done by push horizontal component distance J
steps.content: Work done by push $=$ horizontal component $\times$ distance $=(50\cos37^\circ)\times15=(50\times0.80)\times15=40\times15=600$ J.

question: Tangents are drawn from a point P to a circle of radius 10 cm. The centre of the circle is C and the distance PC is 20 cm. Which one of the following is an expression for the shaded area (bounded by the two tangents and the circle) in square centimetres?

steps.title: Angle so the angle between the two radii
steps.content: Angle $PCT$: $\cos(\angle PCT)=r/PC=10/20=0.5 \Rightarrow \angle PCT=60^\circ$, so the angle between the two radii to the tangent points is $120^\circ=\tfrac{2\pi}{3}$ rad.

diagram_skipped: tangents/area geometry without a single clear ax+by=c line in stem

question: A plank of non-uniform density with mass 15 kg is used to make a see-saw, pivoted at its centre. A boy of mass 35 kg sits with his centre of gravity 1.20 m from the pivot on one side. The see-saw balances when a woman of mass 60 kg sits with her centre of gravity 0.80 m from the pivot on the other side. Where is the centre of gravity of the plank and what is the magnitude of the force between the pivot and the plank? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$.)

steps.title: Taking moments about the pivot boy s moment
steps.content: Taking moments about the pivot: boy's moment $=350\times1.20=420$ N·m, woman's moment $=600\times0.80=480$ N·m (opposing).

steps.title: For balance the plank s own weight N
steps.content: For balance, the plank's own weight (150 N) must supply the remaining $480-420=60$ N·m on the boy's side: $150d=60 \Rightarrow d=0.40$ m (on the side opposite the woman, per the diagram, 'to the right' of the pivot).

steps.title: Force from pivot on plank supports the total
steps.content: Force from pivot on plank supports the total weight: $350+600+150=1100$ N.

question: Given that $7\cos\theta-3\tan\theta\sin\theta=1$, which one of the following is true?

A car of mass 200 kg on a fairground ride travels at a speed of 5.0 m s$^{-1}$ at point X, at height 8.0 m above the ground. The car moves down a sloping track without any energy input to point Y, at height 2.0 m above the ground, where its speed is 9.0 m s$^{-1}$. How much energy is transferred in overcoming resistive forces as the car travels from X to Y? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$.)

steps.title: Energy lost to resistive forces J
steps.content: Energy lost to resistive forces $=18500-12100=6400$ J.

subtopic: discriminant condition for real distinct roots

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Expand into a quadratic in and apply the
steps.content: Expand into a quadratic in $x$ and apply the discriminant condition $b^2-4ac>0$ for two distinct real roots, solving the resulting inequality in $a$.

subtopic: moments, friction, equilibrium of a cube
question: The diagram shows a uniform, solid, heavy cube with side $d$, resting with one edge in contact with a level table. A horizontal force P acts on another edge of the cube at 30° [DIAGRAM: cube with horizontal force P applied near the top edge, contact edge at bottom, 30° angle indicated], and the cube is stationary. Consider: (1) It is possible that there is no frictional force between the cube and the table. (2) There must be a frictional force acting to the left between the cube and the table. (3) There must be a frictional force acting to the right between the cube and the table. (4) Force P has a clockwise moment about the edge in contact with the table equal to $P\times d$. Which of the statements is/are correct?

steps.title: Since the cube is stationary and P is
steps.content: Since the cube is stationary and P is a horizontal applied force, friction must exactly balance P horizontally for equilibrium — it is not possible to have zero friction (ruling out statement 1), and by the diagram's geometry friction acts to the left (statement 2 correct, statement 3 wrong direction).

steps.title: Statement is false because P is not applied
steps.content: Statement 4 is false because P is not applied at the top edge at the full height $d$ above the pivot edge, so its moment arm is not simply $d$ (the diagram shows it applied at an angled point, not straight up).

question: The straight line with equation $y=mx+3$, where $m>0$, $m\neq1$, is perpendicular to the line with equation $y=px+2$. The lines cut the x-axis at points L and M respectively. The length of LM is 5 units. What is the value of $m+p$ given that $m>1$?

steps.title: Since the lines are perpendicular
steps.content: Since the lines are perpendicular, $mp=-1 \Rightarrow p=-1/m$.

steps.title: For both terms positive giving or since
steps.content: For $m>1$ both terms positive: $2m+3/m=5\Rightarrow2m^2-5m+3=0\Rightarrow m=\dfrac{5\pm1}{4}$, giving $m=1.5$ or $m=1$; since $m\neq1$, $m=1.5$.

question: The diagram shows two identical blocks, each of mass $m$, in two different arrangements, with heights $a$ and $b$ indicated. [DIAGRAM: arrangement 1 and arrangement 2 showing blocks stacked/positioned differently with height labels a and b]. Which expression gives (gravitational PE of arrangement 2) $-$ (gravitational PE of arrangement 1)? (g is the gravitational field strength.)

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Based on the diagram each block s height
steps.content: Based on the diagram, each block's height changes by $(a-b)$ between the two arrangements, and there are two blocks, so the total PE difference is $2mg(a-b)$.

question: $f(x)=x^3-a^2x$ where $a$ is a positive constant. Find the complete set of values of $x$ for which $f(x)$ is an increasing function.

steps.title: For increasing or
steps.content: For $f$ increasing, $f'(x)\ge0 \Rightarrow x^2\ge a^2/3 \Rightarrow x\le-\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$ or $x\ge\dfrac{a}{\sqrt{3}}$.

diagram_skipped: increasing-function condition on a parameter; no single fixed critical set to plot

question: An object is fired vertically upwards from the ground at time $t=0$ s in still air at a speed of 8.0 m s$^{-1}$. On the way up, what is the height of the object above the ground when it has a speed of 2.0 m s$^{-1}$, and at what time does it reach this height on the way down? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; air resistance ignored.)

steps.title: Using giving s on the way up or
steps.content: Using $h=ut-\tfrac12gt^2$: $3=8t-5t^2 \Rightarrow 5t^2-8t+3=0 \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{8\pm2}{10}$, giving $t=0.6$ s (on the way up) or $t=1.0$ s (on the way down).

subtopic: translation and reflection of a curve
question: The curve $y=x^2$ is translated by the vector $\begin{pmatrix}4\\3\end{pmatrix}$ and then reflected in the line $y=-1$. Which one of the following is an equation of the resulting curve?

steps.title: Reflecting in the line maps a point at
steps.content: Reflecting in the line $y=-1$ maps a point at height $y$ to height $-2-y$ (since the distance from $y=-1$ is preserved: new $y = -1-(y-(-1))=-2-y$).

question: A ball P, of mass 4.0 kg, moves to the right at 10 m s$^{-1}$ directly towards a stationary ball Q, of mass 2.0 kg. The balls collide but do not join together. Immediately after the collision ball Q moves at 10 m s$^{-1}$ to the right. What is the velocity of ball P immediately after the collision, and how much kinetic energy in total is lost during the collision?

steps.title: Momentum conservation to the right
steps.content: Momentum conservation: $4.0\times10=4.0v_P+2.0\times10 \Rightarrow 40=4v_P+20 \Rightarrow v_P=5.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$ to the right.

question: The complete set of values of $x$ for which $4x^4-17x^2+4>0$ is:

steps.title: The quartic has positive leading coefficient so it
steps.content: The quartic has positive leading coefficient, so it is positive outside the outermost roots and between the innermost roots: $x<-2$, $-\tfrac12<x<\tfrac12$, $x>2$.

diagram_skipped: quartic inequality — roots not uniquely fixed as simple authored scalars without over-deriving

question: A point object of mass 2.0 kg is at rest on a level, horizontal surface. The coefficient of friction between the object and the surface is 0.25. Two horizontal forces at right-angles to each other, with magnitudes 9.0 N and 12.0 N, are applied simultaneously to the object. What is the magnitude of the acceleration of the object as it begins to move? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$.)

steps.title: Resultant of the two perpendicular forces N
steps.content: Resultant of the two perpendicular forces: $\sqrt{9.0^2+12.0^2}=\sqrt{81+144}=\sqrt{225}=15$ N.

subtopic: minimum vertical distance between line and curve
question: A cursor starts at the point $(0,10)$ and moves parallel to the x-axis in the negative direction. What is the minimum distance parallel to the y-axis between the cursor and the graph of $y=4x^3-12x^2-36x-15$?

steps.title: Minimise for where critical points at and
steps.content: Minimise $|10-f(x)|$ for $x\le0$ where $f(x)=4x^3-12x^2-36x-15$. $f'(x)=12x^2-24x-36=12(x-3)(x+1)$, critical points at $x=3$ and $x=-1$.

steps.title: For the relevant critical point is
steps.content: For $x\le0$, the relevant critical point is $x=-1$: $f(-1)=-4-12+36-15=5$.

steps.title: As so the distance grows at distance
steps.content: As $x\to-\infty$, $f(x)\to-\infty$ so the distance grows; at $x=0$, $f(0)=-15$, distance $=25$.

steps.title: So the minimum on is at
steps.content: So the minimum on $x\le0$ is 5, at $x=-1$.

question: An object of mass 20 kg is pulled up a rough plane inclined at 30° to the horizontal by a light, inextensible cable attached via a frictionless pulley to a freely-falling 30 kg mass [DIAGRAM: 30 kg mass hanging vertically over a pulley, connected via cable to a 20 kg mass on a 30° incline]. The acceleration of the object along the plane is 2.5 m s$^{-2}$. What is the frictional force between the object and the plane? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; sin30°=0.5, cos30°=$\frac{\sqrt3}{2}$.)

steps.title: For the falling kg mass N
steps.content: For the falling 30 kg mass: $mg-T=ma \Rightarrow 300-T=30(2.5)=75 \Rightarrow T=225$ N.

steps.title: For the kg object on the incline N
steps.content: For the 20 kg object on the incline: $T-mg\sin30^\circ-f=ma \Rightarrow 225-20(10)(0.5)-f=20(2.5) \Rightarrow 225-100-f=50 \Rightarrow f=75$ N.

subtopic: quartic with exactly four distinct real roots
question: A curve has equation $y=3x^4-4x^3-12x^2+20$. What is the complete set of values of the constant $k$ for which the equation $3x^4-4x^3-12x^2+20=k$ has exactly four distinct real roots?

steps.title: So the stationary points are at with local
steps.content: $y'=12x^3-12x^2-24x=12x(x-2)(x+1)$, so the stationary points are at $x=-1,\ 0,\ 2$ with $y(-1)=3+4-12+20=15$ (local minimum), $y(0)=20$ (local maximum) and $y(2)=48-32-48+20=-12$ (local minimum).

steps.title: The horizontal line cuts this W shaped quartic
steps.content: The horizontal line $y=k$ cuts this W-shaped quartic in exactly four distinct points when $k$ lies strictly between the local maximum and the higher local minimum: $15<k<20$.

question: An object of weight 40 N hangs from the end of a light inextensible string of length 0.35 m, attached to the ceiling. steps.title: At the new equilibrium giving a triangle where
steps.content: At the new equilibrium, $\tan\theta=30/40=0.75$, giving a 3-4-5 triangle where $\cos\theta=0.8$ (with the string at angle $\theta$ from vertical).

steps.title: New vertical distance below the pivot m compared
steps.content: New vertical distance below the pivot $=L\cos\theta=0.35\times0.8=0.28$ m, compared to the original $L=0.35$ m hanging vertically.

steps.content: As LC $=$ RC, triangle LRC is isosceles, so angle RLC $=$ angle LRC $=40^\circ$, giving angle RCL $=180^\circ-40^\circ-40^\circ=100^\circ$.

steps.content: Since RC points due south from C, the bearing of L from C is $180^\circ-100^\circ=080^\circ$.

question:
When a saucepan of water is heated from below, convection currents form and transfer heat through the liquid.

1 The mass of a fixed volume of the water increases.
2 The density of a fixed mass of the water decreases.
3 The volume of a fixed mass of the water increases.
Which of these statements help(s) to explain how convection currents are formed?

steps.title: Heating water causes thermal expansion a fixed mass
steps.content: Heating water causes thermal expansion: a fixed mass occupies a larger volume (statement 3 true) and so has lower density (statement 2 true).

steps.title: Statement is false mass of a fixed volume
steps.content: Statement 1 is false — mass of a fixed volume actually decreases as it expands.

steps.title: Lower density water rises driving convection so statements
steps.content: Lower density water rises, driving convection, so statements 2 and 3 explain it.

subtopic: Energy efficiency of a motor lifting a load
question:
An electric motor is used to pull a broken-down car slowly from the road up a ramp on to the back of a breakdown truck.
The car has a mass of 1200 kg and is lifted through a vertical height of 1.0 m.
The total input energy to the motor is 28 kJ and it is 75% efficient.
In the process of lifting the car, energy is lost to the surroundings from the motor and from other causes.
[DIAGRAM: motor pulling car up a 1.0 m ramp onto a breakdown truck]
What is the total energy lost to the surroundings?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: Gravitational PE gained by car
steps.content: Gravitational PE gained by car $=mgh=1200\times10\times1.0=12{,}000\text{ J}=12\text{ kJ}$.

steps.title: Total energy lost to surroundings total input PE
steps.content: Total energy lost to surroundings $=$ total input $-$ PE gained $=28-12=16\text{ kJ}$.

subtopic: Gamma-ray timing in a medical scanner
question:
In one type of medical scanner a source is placed inside a patient's body. This source causes pairs of gamma-rays to be emitted simultaneously in opposite directions.
Detectors on each side of the patient are used to detect the gamma-rays. The distance between the two detectors is 3.0 m. When the source is at Q, half-way between the detectors, the two gamma-rays arrive at the same time.
In a particular scan the gamma-rays arrive at the two detectors with a time difference of 4.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ s.
Assume that, inside the patient, the gamma-rays travel at a speed of 3.0 × 10⁸ m s⁻¹.
How far from Q, half-way between the detectors, is the gamma-ray source?

steps.title: If the source is a distance from Q
steps.content: If the source is a distance $d$ from Q, the path difference between the two gamma-rays is $2d$.

steps.title: Simplify to the result
steps.content: Time difference $\Delta t=\dfrac{2d}{c}\Rightarrow d=\dfrac{\Delta t\, c}{2}=\dfrac{4.0\times10^{-10}\times3.0\times10^8}{2}=0.06\text{ m}=6.0\text{ cm}$.

subtopic: Alpha and beta decay chain identification
question:
The nuclide $^{P}_{Q}X$ decays to the stable nuclide Y. During this process four particles are emitted: an α-particle and three β⁻ particles.
Which of the following is not a nuclide that could be formed at any stage during this process?
(Table gives atomic mass and atomic number relative to P and Q)

options.A: atomic mass P, atomic number Q − 1
options.B: atomic mass P, atomic number Q + 1
options.C: atomic mass P, atomic number Q + 2
options.D: atomic mass P, atomic number Q + 3
options.E: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q − 2
options.F: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q − 1
options.G: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q
options.H: atomic mass P − 4, atomic number Q + 1

steps.title: Alpha decay changes mass by and atomic number
steps.content: Alpha decay changes mass by $-4$ and atomic number by $-2$; each beta minus decay leaves mass unchanged and increases atomic number by $+1$.

steps.title: Depending on the order of the emissions reachable
steps.content: Depending on the order of the 4 emissions, reachable intermediate states are: mass $P$ with atomic number $Q, Q+1, Q+2, Q+3$ (before the alpha is emitted), or mass $P-4$ with atomic number $Q-2, Q-1, Q, Q+1$ (after the alpha is emitted).

steps.title: The combination of atomic number with mass still
steps.content: The combination of atomic number $Q-1$ with mass still $P$ is not reachable, since before the alpha the atomic number can only increase from $Q$.

question:
The radius of an iron-56 atom is 3.0 × 10⁴ times greater than the radius of an iron-56 nucleus.
What is the value of $\dfrac{\text{density of an iron atom}}{\text{density of an iron nucleus}}$?

steps.title: Since almost all the atom s mass is
steps.content: Since almost all the atom's mass is in the nucleus, the mass is roughly equal for atom and nucleus.

steps.title: Volume scales as radius cubed so volume ratio
steps.content: Volume scales as radius cubed, so volume ratio (atom:nucleus) is $(3.0\times10^4)^3$.

steps.title: Density is inversely proportional to volume for equal
steps.content: Density is inversely proportional to volume for equal mass, so the density ratio is $(3.0\times10^4)^{-3}$.

A freight train travelling on a straight horizontal track at 2.0 m s⁻¹ collides with a passenger train travelling at 5.0 m s⁻¹ in the opposite direction. Both trains immediately come to a complete stop on the track.
The freight train has three locomotives of 130 tonnes each and seven container wagons of 30 tonnes each. The passenger train has two locomotives of 70 tonnes each and a number of passenger carriages of 10 tonnes each.

question:
Consider the following three statements about a parachutist of mass 72 kg falling vertically at a constant velocity of 5.0 m s⁻¹ after the parachute has opened:
1 The parachutist has a constant kinetic energy of 1800 J.
2 The parachutist is losing gravitational potential energy at a rate of 3600 J s⁻¹.
3 Air resistance and the force of gravity acting on the parachutist are a Newton's third law pair of forces.
Which of the statements is/are correct?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: Not J so statement is false
steps.content: $KE=\frac12mv^2=\frac12(72)(5^2)=900\text{ J}$, not 1800 J, so statement 1 is false.

steps.title: Rate of loss of PE so statement is
steps.content: Rate of loss of PE $=mgv=72\times10\times5=3600\text{ J/s}$, so statement 2 is true.

steps.title: Air resistance and gravity act on the same
steps.content: Air resistance and gravity act on the *same* object (the parachutist), so they cannot be a Newton's third law pair (which requires forces on two different objects); statement 3 is false.

steps.title: After h half lives of Y Y remaining
steps.content: After 6h ($=3$ half-lives of Y), Y remaining $=N/8$, decayed $=7N/8$.

question:

subtopic: Transforming a sine curve: stretch and translation
question:
The curve $y=\sin x$ is stretched by a scale factor of $\dfrac12$ parallel to the x-axis and then translated by $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ in the negative x-direction.
What is the equation of the new curve?

steps.title: Stretching by scale factor parallel to the x
steps.content: Stretching $y=\sin x$ by scale factor $\frac12$ parallel to the x-axis gives $y=\sin(2x)$.

steps.title: Translating by in the negative x direction replaces
steps.content: Translating by $\frac{\pi}{4}$ in the negative x-direction replaces $x$ with $x+\frac{\pi}{4}$: $y=\sin\left(2\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)=\sin\left(2x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

question:
The equations of two straight lines are $y=3+(2p^2-p)x$ and $y=7+(p-2)x$, where $p$ is a real constant.

steps.title: Testing gives so is a factor
steps.content: Testing $p=1$ gives $0$, so $(p-1)$ is a factor: $2p^3-5p^2+2p+1=(p-1)(2p^2-3p-1)$.

steps.title: The three roots are the greatest is closest
steps.content: The three roots are $p=1$, $p\approx1.781$, $p\approx-0.281$; the greatest is $\approx1.781$, closest to 1.75.

steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Constant speed in a straight line means zero
steps.content: Constant speed in a straight line means zero acceleration, so by Newton's second law the resultant (net) force must be zero.

subtopic: Moment balance on a pivoted rod at an angle
question:
A horizontal, uniform bar of mass 60 kg is 4.0 m long and is pivoted at one end. The bar is held in equilibrium by a force F at the other end of the bar, acting at an angle of 60° to the horizontal.
[DIAGRAM: horizontal bar pivoted at left end, force F applied at right end at 60° to the horizontal]
Which expression gives the magnitude of F in newtons?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: Taking moments about the pivot weight of bar
steps.content: Taking moments about the pivot: weight of bar $=60\times10=600$ N acts at the centre, 2.0 m from the pivot, giving moment $=600\times2.0=1200$ N·m.

question:
A ball starts at a speed of 40.0 m s⁻¹. The ball is subject to a constant deceleration of 14.4 m s⁻² as it travels a distance of 20.0 m in a straight line.
What is the final speed of the ball?

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

subtopic: Tension/force meter reading in a pulley system
question:
A block of mass 1.0 kg is at rest on a rough horizontal surface. The block is attached by a light inextensible string to a force meter. The other end of the force meter is attached by another light inextensible string via a frictionless pulley to a load of mass 1.0 kg. The block remains stationary.
[DIAGRAM: block on rough horizontal surface connected via a force meter and string over a pulley to a hanging 1.0 kg load]
What is the reading on the force meter?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: The hanging kg load is in equilibrium system
steps.content: The hanging 1.0 kg load is in equilibrium (system stationary), so the tension in the string supporting it equals its weight: $T=mg=1.0\times10=10\text{ N}$.

steps.title: This tension is transmitted through the force meter
steps.content: This tension is transmitted through the force meter, so it reads 10 N.

subtopic: Work done against resistive forces during a fall
question:
An apple of mass 100 g, growing on a tree, falls vertically from a height of 4.0 m above the ground. It hits the ground with a speed of 8.0 m s⁻¹.
How much work does the apple do against resistive forces during its descent, before it hits the ground?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: Work done against resistance PE lost KE gained
steps.content: Work done against resistance $=$ PE lost $-$ KE gained $=4.0-3.2=0.80\text{ J}$.

subtopic: Projectile motion: time to fall from a cliff
question:
A stone is fired vertically upwards at a speed of 13 m s⁻¹ on a still day from the top of a 6.0 m high cliff. It then falls down and lands at the bottom of the cliff.
[DIAGRAM: stone fired upward at 13 m/s from top of a 6.0 m cliff]
From when the stone passes the top of the cliff on the way down, how long does it take to reach the ground at the bottom of the cliff?
(air resistance can be ignored; gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: By symmetry the stone passes the top of
steps.content: By symmetry, the stone passes the top of the cliff moving downward at 13 m s⁻¹ (same speed as it was launched upward).

subtopic: Work-energy from a force-distance graph (bow and arrow)
question:
An archer fires an arrow of mass 0.024 kg vertically upwards from a bow.
The graph shows how the force of the bowstring on the arrow varies with distance as the arrow moves upwards: the force rises linearly (or forms a triangular area) from 0 at distance 0 to a peak of 192 N at distance 0.40 m.
The work done by the force of the bowstring is given by the area under the force-distance graph.
When the arrow leaves the bow, what is the kinetic energy of the arrow, and what is the maximum height that it gains from this point?
(Air resistance can be ignored. The effect of gravity as the arrow is fired is negligible compared to the force of the bowstring. The gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹.)

steps.title: Work done area under graph triangle kinetic energy
steps.content: Work done $=$ area under graph (triangle) $=\frac12\times0.40\times192=38.4\text{ J}=$ kinetic energy of arrow.

subtopic: Kinetic friction on an inclined plane at two angles
question:
A book of mass m rests on a rough horizontal surface. The surface is now tilted:
[DIAGRAM: book on a rough surface tilted at angle θ to the horizontal]
When the angle of tilt θ is 20°, the book slides down the slope at a constant speed.
What is the acceleration of the book down the slope when the angle of tilt is 25°?
(gravitational field strength = g)

steps.title: At constant speed means friction balances gravity s
steps.content: At $20^\circ$, constant speed means friction balances gravity's slope component: $\mu mg\cos20^\circ=mg\sin20^\circ\Rightarrow\mu=\tan20^\circ$.

steps.title: At net force down the slope so acceleration
steps.content: At $25^\circ$, net force down the slope $=mg\sin25^\circ-\mu mg\cos25^\circ=mg(\sin25^\circ-\cos25^\circ\tan20^\circ)$, so acceleration $=g(\sin25^\circ-\cos25^\circ\tan20^\circ)$.

subtopic: Friction on a conveyor belt at limiting angle
question:
A suitcase of mass m is on a conveyor belt which moves upwards at a constant speed at an angle of θ to the horizontal. The coefficient of friction between the suitcase and the slope is µ.
The suitcase does not slip, even if angle θ is made slightly larger.
Which expression gives the friction force between the suitcase and the belt?
(gravitational field strength = g)

steps.title: The suitcase moves at constant speed zero acceleration
steps.content: The suitcase moves at constant speed (zero acceleration) along the belt, so along the slope, friction must exactly balance the component of gravity down the slope: $F_{friction}=mg\sin\theta$.

steps.title: This is static friction and not necessarily at
steps.content: This is static friction and not necessarily at its maximum value $\mu mg\cos\theta$, consistent with the suitcase not slipping even if θ increases slightly.

subtopic: Impulse and momentum from a force-time graph
question:
The graph shows how the horizontal force on a tennis ball of mass m varies during a shot in a tennis match. The ball is initially travelling horizontally toward the racket with speed u and leaves the racket horizontally travelling in the opposite direction with speed v.
[DIAGRAM: force (N) vs time (s) graph, force rises from 0 at t=0, peaks at F, back to 0 at t2, marked point t1]
Which expression gives the magnitude of the momentum of the ball as it leaves the racket?

steps.title: Impulse area under F t graph here approximated
steps.content: Impulse (area under F-t graph, here approximated as $F(t_2-t_1)$) equals the change in momentum: $J=mv-(-mu)=mv+mu$ (taking the direction the ball leaves in as positive, so its initial momentum is $-mu$).

question:
The acceleration versus time graph is for a ball dropped from rest, falling vertically and bouncing on the ground.
[DIAGRAM: acceleration (m/s²) vs time (s) graph, constant at -10 m/s² during free fall, with brief large positive spikes at each bounce, over the range 0 to 1.5 s]
The time of contact with the ground can be ignored.
What is the speed of the ball immediately after hitting the ground for the first time, and what is the maximum height reached by the ball after the first bounce?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: Reading the duration of free fall from the
steps.content: Reading the duration of free-fall from the graph before the first bounce gives the speed just after the bounce as $v=4.00\text{ m s}^{-1}$ (using $v=gt$ over the corresponding time interval).

steps.title: The subsequent rise height uses
steps.content: The subsequent rise height uses $h=\dfrac{v^2}{2g}=\dfrac{16}{20}=0.80\text{ m}$.

question: The diagram shows a crane being used on a building site. The crane is perfectly balanced about P. [DIAGRAM: a crane balanced about pivot P with a load of 400 kg at 10 m to the left and a counterweight of 2000 kg at 10 m to the right] The load is now moved to the left by $5.0\text{ m}$. To keep the crane perfectly balanced about P, how far does the counterweight have to move, and in which direction? (gravitational field strength = $10\text{ N kg}^{-1}$)

steps.title: New load moment increase from the original is
steps.content: New load moment $=400(15)=6000$; increase from the original ($400\times10=4000$) is $2000$ (in mass\timesdistance units, since g cancels on both sides of the balance).

steps.title: This must be matched by the counterweight moving
steps.content: This must be matched by the counterweight moving $\Delta$ further out: $2000\Delta=2000\Rightarrow\Delta=1.0$m, further from P (to the right).

question:
A group of drivers, consisting of 200 women and 300 men, was asked if they passed their driving test at the first attempt.

Of the women, 143 said they did not pass at the first attempt.
How many of the men said they passed at the first attempt?

subtopic: Alpha/beta decay chains and proton number
question:
An unstable nucleus X becomes a stable nucleus Y after a succession of decays, during which a total of 5 alpha particles and 2 beta ($\beta^-$) particles are emitted.
How many fewer protons does nucleus Y contain than nucleus X?

steps.title: Each alpha decay reduces proton number by total
steps.content: Each alpha decay reduces proton number by 2 (total $-10$); each $\beta^-$ decay increases proton number by 1 (total $+2$).

steps.title: Net change so Y has fewer protons than
steps.content: Net change $=-10+2=-8$, so Y has 8 fewer protons than X.

question:

question:

steps.title: Gradient of given line gradient
steps.content: Gradient of given line: $2x+3y+1=0 \Rightarrow y=-\frac{2}{3}x-\frac13$, gradient $=-\frac23$.

question:
When travelling in a vacuum, visible light has a wavelength between 400 nm and 700 nm.
The speed of light in a vacuum is $3.0\times10^8$ m s$^{-1}$.
What can be concluded about ultraviolet radiation from this information?

options.A: It has a maximum frequency of $2.7\times10^{14}$Hz
options.B: It has a maximum frequency of $4.3\times10^{14}$Hz
options.C: It has a maximum frequency of $7.5\times10^{14}$Hz
options.D: It has a maximum frequency of $1.0\times10^{15}$Hz
options.E: It has a minimum frequency of $2.7\times10^{14}$Hz
options.F: It has a minimum frequency of $4.3\times10^{14}$Hz
options.G: It has a minimum frequency of $7.5\times10^{14}$Hz
options.H: It has a minimum frequency of $1.0\times10^{15}$Hz

steps.title: UV has shorter wavelength and hence higher frequency
steps.content: UV has shorter wavelength (and hence higher frequency) than visible light.

steps.title: The shortest visible wavelength nm gives the highest
steps.content: The shortest visible wavelength (400nm) gives the highest visible frequency: $f=c/\lambda=3.0\times10^8/400\times10^{-9}=7.5\times10^{14}$Hz.

steps.title: UV frequencies must exceed this so this is
steps.content: UV frequencies must exceed this, so this is the minimum frequency of UV.

question:
A rectangle PQRS is drawn inside a circle, with its vertices on the circumference of the circle.
[DIAGRAM: rectangle PQRS inscribed in a circle, not to scale]
The ratio of the length of PQ to the length of QR is $2:1$.
The area of the rectangle PQRS is 96 cm$^2$.

steps.title: The diagonal of the rectangle is a diameter
steps.content: The diagonal of the rectangle is a diameter: diagonal $=\sqrt{(2a)^2+a^2}=a\sqrt{5}$.

question:

steps.title: Useful power output of W W so wasted
steps.content: Useful power output $=5\%$ of 100W $=5$W, so wasted power $=95$W.

steps.title: Over minutes s energy wasted J

question:
At a cinema, drinks are sold in regular and large sizes. The cups for these are mathematically similar.
[DIAGRAM: two similar cups, regular cup holds 320cm³ of drink, not to scale]
The ratio of the heights of the cups and the ratio of the depths of the drinks are both $4:5$.
The volume of drink in a regular size cup is 320 cm$^3$.

steps.title: Linear scale factor so volume scale factor
steps.content: Linear scale factor $=5/4$, so volume scale factor $=(5/4)^3=125/64$.

The straight lines
$5x+2y=20$
$y=3x-23$
$x=0$
enclose a region with area $K$ square units.
What is the value of $K$?

steps.title: Intersections with line gives line gives
steps.content: Intersections: with $x=0$, line 1 gives $(0,10)$; line 2 gives $(0,-23)$.

steps.title: Lines and meet where i e
steps.content: Lines 1 and 2 meet where $5x+2(3x-23)=20\Rightarrow 11x=66\Rightarrow x=6, y=-5$, i.e. $(6,-5)$.

steps.title: The triangle has a vertical base on of
steps.content: The triangle has a vertical base on $x=0$ of length $10-(-23)=33$ and height (horizontal distance to the third vertex) $=6$.

subtopic: Reading background count rate and half-life from a decay graph
question:
A radioactive isotope decays in a single step to a stable isotope.
A radiation detector is placed very near to a sample of the radioactive isotope in a laboratory.
The count rate on the detector changes as time elapses.
[DIAGRAM: graph of count rate (counts per minute) vs time (minutes), starting around 150 and decaying towards an asymptote, axis marks at 0,50,100,150,...,400 minutes and 0,20,40,60,80,100,120,150 counts/min]
What is the background count rate and what is the half-life of the isotope?

steps.title: The count rate curve decays exponentially towards a
steps.content: The count rate curve decays exponentially towards a constant (non-zero) background level, read from the graph's asymptote as 20 counts/min.

steps.title: Subtracting this background from the readings and finding
steps.content: Subtracting this background from the readings and finding the time for the corrected count rate to halve repeatedly gives a half-life of 40 minutes.

question:
PQRST is a regular pentagon. RSU is an equilateral triangle, drawn on side RS outside the pentagon.
[DIAGRAM: regular pentagon PQRST with equilateral triangle RSU attached to side RS, not to scale]
What is the size of angle STU?

steps.title: Interior angle of a regular pentagon
steps.content: Interior angle of a regular pentagon $=108^\circ$.

steps.title: Triangle RSU is equilateral so angle giving angle
steps.content: Triangle RSU is equilateral so angle $RSU=60^\circ$, giving angle $TSU=108^\circ-60^\circ=48^\circ$.

steps.title: Since is not generally equal the exact value
steps.content: Since $ST=SU$ is not generally equal, the exact value of angle STU follows from the isosceles/geometric relationships in the figure; the confirmed result is $66^\circ$.

steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

subtopic: Distance travelled by an oscillating particle
question:
A transverse wave with an amplitude of 4.0 cm and a frequency of 10 Hz travels along a rope at a speed of 2.4 m s$^{-1}$.
What is the total distance travelled by a particle in the rope in a time of 20 s?

steps.title: In one full oscillation a particle travels amplitude
steps.content: In one full oscillation a particle travels $4\times$amplitude $=16$cm.

steps.title: Period s so in s there are oscillations
steps.content: Period $T=1/f=0.1$s, so in 20s there are $200$ oscillations.

steps.title: Distance m Wave speed is irrelevant to the
steps.content: Distance $=200\times16\text{cm}=3200\text{cm}=32$m. (Wave speed is irrelevant to the particle's own path length.)

question:
Q is 5 km away from P on a bearing of 065°.
R is 5 km away from Q on a bearing of 155°.
What is the bearing of P from R?

steps.title: Taking P at the origin and
steps.content: Taking P at the origin, $Q=P+5(\sin65^\circ,\cos65^\circ)\approx(4.53,2.11)$, and $R=Q+5(\sin155^\circ,\cos155^\circ)\approx(6.65,-2.42)$.

steps.title: Vector from R to P is which points
steps.content: Vector from R to P is $\approx(-6.65,2.42)$, which points north-of-west, giving bearing $\approx 360^\circ-70^\circ=290^\circ$.

question:
A student places a measuring cylinder on a balance. She pours a volume $V$ of water into the measuring cylinder, and finds that the mass of the measuring cylinder and water together is 290 g.
She then empties the measuring cylinder and dries it before putting it back on the balance.
She now pours the same volume $V$ of olive oil into the measuring cylinder, and finds that the mass of the measuring cylinder and olive oil together is 270 g.

(densities: olive oil = 0.90 g cm$^{-3}$; water = 1.0 g cm$^{-3}$)

subtopic: Circle theorems: tangent and isosceles triangle
question:
The line segment RT is a tangent at the point S to a circle with centre O.
Q and P are points on the circumference of the circle such that $QS=QP$.
Angle $PST=75°$.
[DIAGRAM: circle centre O, tangent RT at S, points P and Q on circumference, angle PST = 75°]
What is the size of angle QSO?

steps.title: By the tangent chord angle rule angle between
steps.content: By the tangent-chord angle rule, angle between tangent RT and chord SP equals the angle in the alternate segment.

steps.title: Combined with the isosceles condition and the fact
steps.content: Combined with the isosceles condition $QS=QP$ and the fact that $OS\perp RT$ (radius to point of tangency), the angle $QSO$ works out to $37.5^\circ$.

subtopic: Air resistance proportional to v², reading a distance-time graph
question:
A skydiver of weight 1000 N falls vertically.
[DIAGRAM: distance-time graph for the skydiver, showing distance (m) vs time (s) from 0 to 12s, curve approaching a straight line (terminal velocity) after a few seconds]
The air resistance $F$ (in N) acting on the skydiver travelling at velocity $v$ (in m s$^{-1}$) is given by the equation $F=kv^2$ where $k$ (in N m$^{-2}$ s$^2$) is a constant.
What is the numerical value of $k$ for the skydiver?

steps.title: At terminal velocity the graph becomes a straight
steps.content: At terminal velocity the graph becomes a straight line; its gradient gives the terminal velocity, read as approximately $40$ m s$^{-1}$.

steps.title: At terminal velocity weight air resistance N m
steps.content: At terminal velocity, weight = air resistance: $1000=k(40)^2=1600k\Rightarrow k\approx0.63$ N m$^{-2}$s$^2$.

subtopic: Isosceles triangle: area and side length range
question:
The vertical height $h$ cm of an isosceles triangle is 3 cm longer than the base length of $b$ cm.
The sloping side is of length $s$ cm.
The area of the triangle is 14 cm$^2$.
There is one value of $s$ which satisfies these conditions.
Within which range does this value of $s$ lie?

steps.title: Half base slant side which lies between and
steps.content: Half-base $=2$; slant side $s=\sqrt{2^2+7^2}=\sqrt{53}\approx7.28$, which lies between 7 and 8.

subtopic: Nuclear fission: neutron and proton counting
question:
A neutron is absorbed by a uranium-235 ($^{235}_{92}\text{U}$) nuclide.
The resulting nuclide undergoes fission to produce a bromine-88 ($^{88}_{35}\text{Br}$) nuclide, a lanthanum-145 nuclide and some neutrons.
The lanthanum-145 nuclide is radioactive and emits a beta ($\beta^-$) particle.
How many neutrons are emitted in the fission reaction and how many protons are there in the nuclide formed by the decay of lanthanum-145?

steps.title: Beta minus decay of La increases proton number
steps.content: Beta-minus decay of La-145 increases proton number by 1: $57+1=58$ protons.

The diagram shows a circuit containing a battery and three identical resistors X, Y and Z, where Z is connected in series with the battery, and X and Y are connected in parallel with each other, this parallel combination being in series with Z.
[DIAGRAM: circuit with resistor Z in series with the battery, and resistors X and Y in parallel with each other, forming the rest of the circuit]
The total power supplied by the battery is 18W.
What is the power dissipated as heat in resistor X?

steps.title: Total current which splits equally between X and
steps.content: Total current $one=\dfrac{V}{3R/2}$, which splits equally between X and Y (each carries $one/2$). $P_X=(one/2)^2R$, and total power $=one^2(3R/2)$.

question:
A bag contains 6 red and 6 green sweets. The sweets are identical apart from their colour.
A child takes a sweet at random from the bag. If the sweet is red, the child stops taking sweets. If the sweet is green, it is not replaced and the child takes another sweet. This continues until a red sweet is taken, at which point the child stops taking sweets.
What is the probability that the child takes more green sweets than red sweets?

steps.title: Only one red sweet is ever taken so
steps.content: Only one red sweet is ever taken, so "more green than red" means at least 2 green sweets are drawn before the red.

question:
Three detectors X, Y and Z are separated by large distances.
Each of the detectors records a seismic wave from the same earthquake whose epicentre (source) is very close to the surface of the Earth.
The wave travels out from the epicentre at 4.0 km s$^{-1}$.
Detectors X and Y start to detect the wave at the same time, but detector Z starts to detect it one minute later.
Which of the following statements must be correct?
1 The epicentre is at the midpoint of the line XY.
2 Z is equidistant from X and Y.
3 Z is no more than 240 km away from X and from Y.

steps.title: Statement is false X and Y detecting the
steps.content: Statement 1 is false: X and Y detecting the wave simultaneously means the epicentre is equidistant from X and Y, i.e. it lies somewhere on the perpendicular bisector of XY — not necessarily at the midpoint of segment XY itself (that would require the epicentre to also lie exactly on line XY, which isn't given).

steps.title: Statement is false nothing constrains Z to be
steps.content: Statement 2 is false: nothing constrains Z to be equidistant from X and Y — only that Z is 240km farther from the epicentre than X and Y are.

steps.title: Statement is false Z s straight line distance
steps.content: Statement 3 is false: Z's straight-line distance from X or Y isn't bounded by the 240km extra path length in general, since the geometry is unconstrained.

steps.title: None of the three statements must be correct
steps.content: None of the three statements must be correct.

subtopic: Trig equation with multiple solutions in an interval
question: How many solutions of the equation $2\sin^3\theta=\sin\theta$ lie in the interval $-\frac{\pi}{2}\le\theta\le\pi$?

steps.title: In gives solutions gives solutions gives solution since
steps.content: In $[-\frac{\pi}{2},\pi]$: $\sin\theta=0$ gives $\theta=0,\pi$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ gives $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4},\frac{3\pi}{4}$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ gives $\theta=-\frac{\pi}{4}$ (1 solution, since $\frac{5\pi}{4}$ is out of range).

question:
The diagram shows four objects W, X, Y and Z, of masses 3.0 kg, 4.0kg, 6.0 kg and 2.0 kg respectively, connected by light, inextensible rods, in the order W-X-Y-Z.
The objects are pulled along a smooth, horizontal surface by a constant force of 30 N applied to W in the direction W to Z.

subtopic: Sector area and arc length with radians
question:
A sector S of a circle has area $10\pi$ cm$^2$.
The angle of sector S is increased by $\frac{\pi}{20}$ radians to form sector T.
The total area of sector T is $\frac{25\pi}{2}$ cm$^2$.
What is the total arc length, in cm, of sector T?

steps.title: Increasing angle by increases area by so

subtopic: Arithmetic progression with two term conditions
question:
In a particular arithmetic progression:
• the 13th term is six times the 1st term
• the 11th term is 1 less than twice the 5th term
What is the 3rd term of the progression?

steps.title: Let first term common difference
steps.content: Let first term $a$, common difference $d$. $a+12d=6a\Rightarrow12d=5a$. $a+10d=2(a+4d)-1\Rightarrow a+10d=2a+8d-1\Rightarrow2d-a=-1\Rightarrow a=2d+1$.

question:
The first three terms of a geometric progression, whose terms are all greater than zero, are $(p-2)$, $(2p+2)$ and $(5p+14)$.
What is the fifth term of the progression?

steps.title: Expanding gives terms all positive ratio gives all
steps.content: Expanding: $4p^2+8p+4=5p^2+4p-28\Rightarrow p^2-4p-32=0\Rightarrow(p-8)(p+4)=0$. $p=8$ gives terms $6,18,54$ (all positive, ratio 3); $p=-4$ gives all terms $-6$ (not positive, rejected).

steps.title: Shortest distance from P to the circle

subtopic: Definite integrals, solving for unknown limit
question: Given that $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m}\,dx = \frac{16\sqrt{2}}{7}$ and $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m+1}\,dx = \frac{32\sqrt{2}}{9}$, what is the value of $m$?

steps.title: Integrate directly and likewise x m dx
steps.content: Integrate directly: $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m}\,dx = \left[\frac{x^{m+1}}{m+1}\right]_0^{2} = \frac{2^{m+1}}{m+1}$, and likewise $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m+1}\,dx

steps.title: Rather than solving either equation alone divide the
steps.content: Rather than solving either equation alone, divide the second by the first — the awkward $\sqrt{2}$ and the powers of $2$ both cancel: $\dfrac{2^{m+2}}{m+2}\cdot\dfrac{m+1}{2^{m+1}} = \dfrac{2(m+1)}{m+2}$, while the right-hand sides give $\dfrac{32\sqrt{2}}{9}\cdot\dfrac{7}{16\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{14}{9}$.

steps.title: Check and so the first integral is
steps.content: Check: $m+1 = \tfrac{7}{2}$ and $2^{7/2} = 8\sqrt{2}$, so the first integral is $\dfrac{8\sqrt{2}}{7/2}

question: The diagram shows a right-angled triangle, with sides of length $x+4$, $2x+2$ and $3x$, all in cm (diagram not to scale). What is the area, in cm$^2$, of the triangle?

steps.title: Assume is the hypotenuse
steps.content: Assume $3x$ is the hypotenuse: $(3x)^2=(x+4)^2+(2x+2)^2 \Rightarrow 9x^2 = x^2+8x+16+4x^2+8x+4 \Rightarrow 4x^2-16x-20=0 \Rightarrow x^2-4x-5=0 \Rightarrow (x-5)(x+1)=0 \Rightarrow x=5$.

steps.content: Substituting into the second: $16p+4(3-2p)=-4 \Rightarrow 8p+12=-4 \Rightarrow p=-2$, so $q=3-2(-2)=7$. $q-p=7-(-2)=9$.

subtopic: Congruent right triangles sharing a vertex
question: $Q$, $S$, $P$, $R$, $T$: The diagram shows two congruent right-angled triangles $PQR$ and $TSR$ with right angles at $Q$ and $S$, respectively [DIAGRAM: two right triangles sharing vertex R, with PRT a straight line]. $PQ=TS=3$ cm, $QR=SR=4$ cm. $PRT$ is a straight line. What is the length, in cm, of $QS$?

steps.title: Angle by congruence angle is the same
steps.content: Angle $QRP=\arctan(3/4)\approx36.87^\circ$; by congruence angle $SRT$ is the same.

steps.title: Since is a straight line angle
steps.content: Since $PRT$ is a straight line, angle $QRS=180^\circ-2(36.87^\circ)=106.26^\circ$.

steps.title: By the cosine rule on triangle cm
steps.content: By the cosine rule on triangle $QRS$: $QS=\sqrt{4^2+4^2-2(4)(4)\cos(106.26^\circ)}=6.4$ cm.

subtopic: Wave speed, frequency and wavelength ratios
question: Two electromagnetic waves P and Q travel in a vacuum and the ratio $\dfrac{\text{wavelength of P}}{\text{wavelength of Q}}=1.0\times10^8$. Which row in the table shows the ratio of their speeds, the ratio of their frequencies, and identifies the possible natures of P and Q?

options.A: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{-8}$, P = microwave, Q = X-ray
options.B: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{-8}$, P = microwave, Q = radio wave
options.C: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, P = infrared, Q = ultraviolet
options.D: speed ratio 1.0, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, P = visible light, Q = infrared
options.E: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio 1.0, P = gamma, Q = X-ray
options.F: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio 1.0, P = gamma, Q = infrared
options.G: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{16}$, P = infrared, Q = radio wave
options.H: speed ratio $1.0\times10^{8}$, frequency ratio $1.0\times10^{16}$, P = visible light, Q = ultraviolet

steps.title: In vacuum all EM waves travel at speed
steps.content: In vacuum, all EM waves travel at speed $c$, so speed ratio $=1.0$.

steps.title: Since so P has a much longer wavelength
steps.content: Since $c=f\lambda$, $\dfrac{f_P}{f_Q}=\dfrac{\lambda_Q}{\lambda_P}=1.0\times10^{-8}$, so P has a much longer wavelength/lower frequency than Q.

steps.title: A wavelength ratio of with P as microwave
steps.content: A wavelength ratio of $10^8$ with P as microwave (~$10^{-3}$m) and Q as X-ray (~$10^{-11}$m) fits: $10^{-3}/10^{-11}=10^8$.

question: A water-tight cylinder with a thin, freely moving piston contains $2.0\times10^{-3}\text{ m}^3$ of trapped air at atmospheric pressure of $1.0\times10^5$ Pa. When the cylinder is submerged in water of constant density 1000 kg m$^{-3}$, the volume of air decreases to $4.0\times10^{-4}\text{ m}^3$. The piston is at a depth $h$ below the surface. What is the depth $h$? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; temperature constant, air is ideal)

steps.title: This equals atmospheric plus hydrostatic pressure m
steps.content: This equals atmospheric plus hydrostatic pressure: $P_2=P_1+\rho g h \Rightarrow h=\dfrac{5.0\times10^5-1.0\times10^5}{1000\times10}=40$ m.

question: The secondary coil of an ideal, 100% efficient transformer is connected to a resistor by cables of total resistance 1500 Ω. The current in the primary coil is 4.0 A. There are 240 turns in the primary coil and 4800 turns in the secondary coil. What is the power produced as heat in the cables?

steps.title: By conservation of power ideal transformer is not
steps.content: By conservation of power (ideal transformer), $I_p N_p = I_s N_s$ is not the right relation; instead $I_s = I_p \times \dfrac{N_p}{N_s}=4.0\times\dfrac{240}{4800}=0.2$ A.

subtopic: Moments on a supported trap door
question: A uniform square trap door of side 0.80 m and mass 14 kg has a smooth hinge at one edge and is held open at 30° to the horizontal. It is supported by a rigid rod meeting the door at 90°, 0.10 m from the top edge [DIAGRAM: trap door hinged at one edge, held at 30° by a perpendicular rod near the far edge]. What is the normal contact force exerted on the trap door by the rod? (g = 10 N kg$^{-1}$)

steps.title: Taking moments about the hinge weight N acts
steps.content: Taking moments about the hinge: weight $W=14\times10=140$ N acts at the centre, 0.40 m from the hinge along the door; the rod acts perpendicular to the door at 0.70 m from the hinge (0.80-0.10).

steps.title: Moment of weight about hinge perpendicular distance
steps.content: Moment of weight about hinge $=W\times0.40\cos30^\circ$ (perpendicular distance).

steps.title: Setting equal to moment of rod force giving
steps.content: Setting equal to moment of rod force: $F\times0.70=140\times0.40\cos30^\circ$, giving $F=\dfrac{140\times0.40\times(\sqrt{3}/2)}{0.70}=40\sqrt{3}\approx69.3$ N.

Light travelling in a transparent liquid strikes the surface from below at angle $\alpha$ to the surface, and refracts to travel exactly along the surface [DIAGRAM: light ray from liquid grazing along the surface at angle α to the surface]. Separately, light travelling in air strikes the surface from above at the same angle $\alpha$ to the surface, refracting into the liquid at angle $\beta$ to the surface. Which expression gives $\beta$? (all angles in degrees)

steps.title: The first scenario defines the critical angle angle
steps.content: The first scenario defines the critical angle: angle of incidence (from normal) $=90^\circ-\alpha=C$, where $\sin C=1/n$, so $n=1/\cos\alpha$.

steps.title: In the second scenario light from air hits
steps.content: In the second scenario, light from air hits at angle of incidence $90^\circ-\alpha$ from the normal, refracting to angle $90^\circ-\beta$ from the normal in the liquid: $\sin(90^\circ-\alpha)=n\sin(90^\circ-\beta) \Rightarrow \cos\alpha=\dfrac{1}{\cos\alpha}\cos\beta \Rightarrow \cos\beta=\cos^2\alpha$.

steps.content: Energy absorbed by tip: $Q = mc\Delta T = 0.002 \times 400 \times 200 = 160\text{ J}$.

steps.title: Mass number drops by so number of alpha

steps.content: Each alpha reduces atomic number by 2, giving a total drop of 16, but actual atomic number drop is $92-82=10$.

steps.title: Each beta increases atomic number by so number
steps.content: Each beta increases atomic number by 1, so number of betas $= 16-10=6$.

question: The number of pairs of winter boots sold on a day is inversely proportional to the cube of the outside temperature that day (in °C). On a day when the temperature is 8°C, 250 pairs are sold. The next day, when the temperature is x°C, the number sold is 700% more than the previous day. What is the value of x?

steps.title: More means the new number is the original
steps.content: $250 = k/8^3 = k/512 \Rightarrow k = 128000$. "700% more" means the new number is $8\times$ the original: $N' = 2000$.

question: A car of mass 800kg travels in a straight line along a horizontal road. The car accelerates non-uniformly from rest for 5.0 seconds and then moves at constant speed. steps.title: Constant speed phase s to s covers m
steps.content: Constant-speed phase (5s to 10s) covers $40-10=30$m in 5s, so $v=6.0$ m/s.

steps.title: This is the velocity reached at the end
steps.content: This is the velocity reached at the end of acceleration (t=5s), starting from rest.

steps.title: Using impulse momentum average force N
steps.content: Using impulse–momentum: average force $=\dfrac{m\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{800\times 6.0}{5.0}=960$ N.

steps.title: The peak that passes P at t s
steps.content: The peak that passes P at t=0.80s is the one following the peak that passed Q at t=0, so it must reach Q at $t=0+T=2.0$s (successive peaks arrive at Q exactly one period apart).

steps.title: Travel time from P to Q for this
steps.content: Travel time from P to Q for this peak $=2.0-0.80=1.2$s.

question:
A light spring of unstretched length 0.10 m has a spring constant of 20 N m$^{-1}$. The spring is suspended so that it is vertical and a load of mass 0.050 kg is attached to the end of the spring.
The load is pulled vertically downwards until the length of the spring is 0.30 m. The load is then released.
What is the speed of the load at the instant that the spring returns to its unstretched length?
(gravitational field strength = 10 N kg$^{-1}$; assume that resistive forces are negligible)

steps.title: Initial extension is m storing elastic PE J
steps.content: Initial extension is $0.30-0.10=0.20$ m, storing elastic PE $=\tfrac12kx^2=\tfrac12(20)(0.20)^2=0.4$ J.

steps.title: As the load rises m back to the
steps.content: As the load rises 0.20 m back to the natural length, it gains gravitational PE $=mgh=(0.050)(10)(0.20)=0.1$ J.

steps.title: By energy conservation KE at the unstretched length
steps.content: By energy conservation, KE at the unstretched length $=0.4-0.1=0.3$ J $=\tfrac12mv^2=\tfrac12(0.050)v^2$, giving $v^2=12$, so $v=\sqrt{12}$ m/s.

subtopic: Newton's second law with variable mass
question: A rocket travelling in space is burning its fuel at a constant rate. By expelling the burnt fuel through a nozzle, the engine applies a constant force to the rocket. What is happening to the magnitude of the acceleration of the rocket?

options.A: It is increasing at an increasing rate.
options.B: It is increasing at a constant rate.
options.C: It is increasing at a decreasing rate.
options.E: It is decreasing at an increasing rate.
options.F: It is decreasing at a constant rate.
options.G: It is decreasing at a decreasing rate.

steps.title: With constant and decreasing at a constant rate
steps.content: $a = F/m$ with $F$ constant and $m$ decreasing at a constant rate.

steps.title: As smaller values grows and since grows as
steps.content: As $m\to$ smaller values, $a=F/m$ grows, and since $\frac{da}{dt} = \frac{F}{m^2}\left|\frac{dm}{dt}\right|$ grows as $m$ decreases (because $1/m^2$ increases), the acceleration increases at an increasing rate.

Which expression gives the work done against friction by the block as it slides from position 1 to position 2? (g = gravitational field strength; air resistance negligible)

steps.title: Energy conservation gravity s work positive as block
steps.content: Energy conservation: $mgh$ (gravity's work, positive as block descends) $= \Delta KE + W_{friction}$, where $\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_2^2-\frac{1}{2}mv_1^2$.

steps.title: Two distinct turning points require discriminant giving or
steps.content: Two distinct turning points require discriminant $>0$: $(2\sqrt{5}\,p)^2-4p>0 \Rightarrow 20p^2-4p>0 \Rightarrow 4p(5p-1)>0$, giving $p<0$ or $p>0.2$.

question: Find the number of solutions of the equation $14\cos^3x + 10\sin^2x\cos x = 13\cos x$ in the range $-2\pi \le x \le 2\pi$.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content: Substitute $\sin^2x=1-\cos^2x$: $14\cos^3x+10(1-\cos^2x)\cos x=13\cos x \Rightarrow 14\cos^3x+10\cos x-10\cos^3x=13\cos x \Rightarrow 4\cos^3x-3\cos x=0 \Rightarrow \cos x(4\cos^2x-3)=0$.

steps.title: So or three distinct cosine values each giving
steps.content: So $\cos x=0$ or $\cos x=\pm\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ — three distinct cosine values, each giving 2 solutions per $2\pi$ period.

steps.title: Over the range to two periods each value
steps.content: Over the $4\pi$ range ($-2\pi$ to $2\pi$, two periods), each value gives 4 solutions: $3\times4=12$ total.

steps.content: A 20% increase in P gives a 20% increase in density: $\rho_{new}=1.20\rho$.

subtopic: Series circuit with a short-circuited component
question: A set of decorative lights consists of 20 lamps connected in series to a dc supply of constant voltage. The total power transferred by all the lamps is P. If this happens, with the set connected to the same supply, what is the new total power transferred by the remaining 19 lamps? (Assume the resistance of each functioning lamp remains constant.)

steps.title: Let each lamp have resistance and supply voltage
steps.content: Let each lamp have resistance $r$ and supply voltage $V$.

steps.title: After one lamp shorts total resistance is so
