[DIAGRAM: A diagram represents the structure of a charged atom (ion) of one isotope of an element, showing the nucleus (protons and neutrons) and surrounding electrons. Answer options A-G show different nucleus/electron configurations.]
The diagram represents the structure of a charged atom (ion) of one isotope of an element. Which diagram represents the structure of an oppositely charged ion of a different isotope of the same element?

options.A: [diagram: 3 unshaded + 4 shaded nucleons; 1 electron on inner shell]
options.B: [diagram: 3 unshaded + 3 shaded nucleons; 2 electrons on inner shell]
options.C: [diagram: 4 unshaded + 3 shaded nucleons; 2 electrons on inner shell]
options.D: [diagram: 3 unshaded + 5 shaded nucleons; 3 electrons (2 inner + 1 outer)]
options.E: [diagram: 5 unshaded + 3 shaded nucleons; 3 electrons (2 inner + 1 outer)]
options.F: [diagram: 3 unshaded + 5 shaded nucleons; 5 electrons (2 inner + 3 outer)]
options.G: [diagram: 3 unshaded + 4 shaded nucleons; 2 electrons on inner shell]

steps.title: Read the given ion
steps.content: In the given diagram, unshaded nucleons are protons and shaded nucleons are neutrons: $3$ protons, $4$ neutrons, and $4$ electrons. Net charge is $-1$ (an anion of the mass-$7$ isotope).

steps.title: State the required species
steps.content: An oppositely charged ion of the same element needs $3$ protons (same atomic number) but fewer than $3$ electrons (net positive charge), and a neutron count other than $4$ (different isotope).

steps.title: Match the diagram counts
steps.content: The matching diagram has $3$ protons, $3$ neutrons, and $2$ electrons: net charge $+1$, mass number $6$. That is a cation of a different isotope of the same element.

subtopic: kinetic theory of gases and pressure
question: A sample of an ideal gas is sealed in a cylindrical container by a piston. The particles of the gas are moving with an average speed $v$, and collide with the surface of the piston with a frequency $f$. The piston is now slowly pushed into the cylinder until the gas occupies half of its original volume, but the gas remains at the same temperature. What is the new average speed of the particles of the gas, and at what frequency do they now collide with the surface of the piston?

steps.title: Since temperature is unchanged the average kinetic energy
steps.content: Since temperature is unchanged, the average kinetic energy (and hence average speed) of the particles is unchanged: speed remains $v$.

steps.title: Halving the volume doubles the particle density so
steps.content: Halving the volume doubles the particle density, so particles collide with the piston twice as often: frequency $=2f$.

question:
[DIAGRAM: A water wave travels from region X into region Y where wave speed differs. Wave peaks separated by one wavelength are shown, with angle θ between wave peaks and the boundary in region X, and angle φ between wave peaks and the boundary in region Y.]
A water wave is travelling in a shallow tank of water. The wave passes from region X into region Y where the speed of the wave differs from that in region X. In region X, the angle between the wave peaks and the boundary between the regions is θ. In region Y, the angle between the wave peaks and the boundary is φ. What are the angle of incidence and the angle of refraction, and in which region is the speed of the wave greater?

steps.title: The angle between the wave peaks wavefront and
steps.content: The angle between the wave peaks (wavefront) and the boundary equals the angle of incidence/refraction (measured from the boundary, not the normal, in this diagram convention), so angle of incidence $=\theta$, angle of refraction $=\phi$.

steps.title: Since the wave peaks are further apart where
steps.content: Since the wave peaks are further apart where the wave travels faster, and region X shows the larger peak spacing based on the diagram, the wave speed is greater in region X.

question: Uranium-238 ($^{238}_{92}$U) decays by a series of alpha and beta ($\beta^-$) emissions to become the stable isotope lead-206 ($^{206}_{82}$Pb). How many beta ($\beta^-$) particles are emitted in the decay of one uranium-238 nucleus to lead-206?

steps.title: Mass number decreases by so there are alpha
steps.content: Mass number decreases by $238-206=32$, so there are $32/4=8$ alpha particles emitted (each reduces mass number by 4 and atomic number by 2).

steps.title: To reach for lead beta particles must be
steps.content: To reach $Z=82$ for lead, $82-76=6$ beta particles must be emitted (each increases $Z$ by 1).

question:
In a laboratory, liquid nitrogen is stored at a very low temperature in a vacuum-walled vessel. The vessel has a double wall made from a poor thermal conductor, with a vacuum in the gap between the two walls. The inner surface of the inner wall is shiny, and the outer surface of the outer wall is shiny. These features insulate the liquid nitrogen by reducing the rate at which thermal energy is transferred to the liquid nitrogen. Which of the following statements explain(s) why these features help to insulate the liquid nitrogen?
1. The shiny inner surface of the inner wall is a good emitter of thermal radiation.
2. Thermal radiation cannot travel in a vacuum.
3. The shiny outer surface of the outer wall is a poor absorber of radiation.

steps.title: Statement is false a shiny surface is a
steps.content: Statement 1 is false: a shiny surface is a poor emitter of thermal radiation (dull/black surfaces are good emitters).

steps.title: Statement is false thermal radiation can travel through
steps.content: Statement 2 is false: thermal radiation can travel through a vacuum (that's how sunlight reaches Earth).

steps.title: Statement is true shiny surfaces are poor absorbers
steps.content: Statement 3 is true: shiny surfaces are poor absorbers of radiation, which reduces heat entering from outside.

question: The primary coil of an ideal, 100% efficient transformer is connected to a 240V mains supply. A lamp L that is connected to the secondary coil has a voltage of 12V across it. An identical lamp and a switch S are also connected in parallel to the transformer's secondary coil (in series with the switch). With the switch open, the current in the primary coil is 0.10A. The switch is now closed. What is the current in the primary coil now and what is the current in lamp L?

steps.title: With switch open power in W power in
steps.content: With switch open, power in $=240\times0.10=24$W $=$ power in lamp L, so $I_L=24/12=2.0$A.

steps.title: Closing the switch adds an identical lamp in
steps.content: Closing the switch adds an identical lamp in parallel (same 12V), doubling total secondary current to 4.0A and total power to 48W.

steps.title: Current in lamp L is unaffected still A
steps.content: Current in lamp L is unaffected (still 2.0A, since it's identical and sees the same voltage).

question: A dc electricity transmission system uses an undersea cable to send electricity from one country to another. On a particular day, the first country supplies electricity at a voltage of 400 kV and 2000A to the transmission system. The second country receives electricity from the transmission system at 160 kV and 4000A. What is the percentage efficiency of the system and how much energy is wasted every minute?

steps.title: Power wasted W energy wasted per minute J
steps.content: Power wasted $=1.6\times10^8$W; energy wasted per minute $=1.6\times10^8\times60=9.6\times10^9$J.

question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph showing thermistor resistance (Ω) vs temperature (°C), decreasing from about 400Ω at 10°C down to about 40Ω at 80°C.]
A circuit contains a 12V battery, a thermistor and a fixed resistor connected in series. The graph shows how the resistance of the thermistor varies with temperature. When the temperature of the thermistor is 10°C the current in the circuit is 25mA. What is the current when the temperature of the thermistor is 80°C?

steps.title: Reading the graph the thermistor resistance at C
steps.content: Reading the graph, the thermistor resistance at 10^\circ\mathrm{C} is about 400Ω, so the fixed resistor is $480-400=80\Omega$.

steps.title: At C the thermistor resistance drops to about
steps.content: At 80^\circ\mathrm{C} the thermistor resistance drops to about 40Ω (from graph), giving total resistance $=40+80=120\Omega$.

subtopic: force on a current-carrying rod in a magnetic field
question:
[DIAGRAM: Two fixed horizontal metal rails, 12cm apart, viewed from above, connected to a dc power supply via a switch. A freely moveable metal rod of length 20cm lies across the rails at 90°. The whole arrangement sits in a uniform magnetic field of 0.50T directed into the page.]
Two fixed horizontal metal rails are side by side and 12cm apart, connected to a dc power supply by a switch that is initially open. A freely moveable metal rod of length 20cm is placed on the rails at 90° to them. The whole arrangement is placed in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude 0.50T directed perpendicularly into the page. The moveable rod has a weight of 0.40N. The switch is now closed, giving a current of 2.4A in the circuit, and the rod moves. What is the initial magnitude of the acceleration of the rod and what is its direction? (gravitational field strength = 10 N kg$^{-1}$)

steps.title: The force on the rod uses the length
steps.content: The force on the rod uses the length of rod between the rails: $F=BIL=0.50\times2.4\times0.12=0.144$N.

steps.title: By the diagram s current field orientation right
steps.content: By the diagram's current/field orientation (right-hand rule), the direction is to the right.

question: Visible light waves are electromagnetic waves that travel through a vacuum at 300 000 km/s with wavelengths that range from 400 nm to 750 nm. The electromagnetic waves emitted by a source are all at frequencies between $6.0\times10^{12}$Hz and $6.0\times10^{14}$Hz. Which statement about the waves emitted by the source is correct?

options.A: Infrared waves are emitted, but not ultraviolet or visible light waves.
options.B: Infrared and visible light waves are emitted, but not ultraviolet waves.
options.C: Infrared, ultraviolet and visible light waves are all emitted.
options.D: Ultraviolet waves are emitted, but not infrared or visible light waves.
options.E: Ultraviolet and visible light waves are emitted, but not infrared waves.

steps.title: Using at nm Hz at nm Hz
So visible light spans to Hz
The source s range to Hz overlaps with
steps.content: The source's range ($6\times10^{12}$ to $6\times10^{14}$Hz) overlaps with the lower part of the visible range (up to $6\times10^{14}$Hz, which is within visible) and extends below $4\times10^{14}$Hz into the infrared.

steps.title: Since the source s maximum frequency Hz is
steps.content: Since the source's maximum frequency ($6\times10^{14}$Hz) is below the visible maximum ($7.5\times10^{14}$Hz), no ultraviolet is emitted.

question: Two trolleys are moving towards each other along a straight horizontal track. One trolley has mass 8.0kg and is travelling to the right at 4.0 m/s. The other trolley has mass 2.0kg and is travelling to the left at 1.0 m/s. When the trolleys collide they stick together. How much kinetic energy is transferred to other forms of energy in the collision?

steps.title: Momentum conservation right positive kg m s
Combined mass kg so m s

question:
[DIAGRAM: Distance-time graph for a car; curved from 0 to (5s, 10m) representing non-uniform acceleration from rest, then a straight line to (10s, 40m) representing constant speed.]
A car of mass 800kg travels in a straight line along a horizontal road. The car accelerates non-uniformly from rest for 5.0 seconds (reaching 10m) and then moves at constant speed (reaching 40m at t=10s). What is the average resultant force acting on the car over the time for which it is accelerating?

steps.title: The constant speed after t s is m
steps.content: The constant speed after t=5s is $\frac{40-10}{10-5}=6.0$m/s.

steps.title: Using impulse momentum theorem average force N
steps.content: Using impulse-momentum theorem, average force $=\frac{m\Delta v}{\Delta t}=\frac{800\times6.0}{5.0}=960$N.

subtopic: density and mass of packed cubes
question: Cubes of side 2.0cm are tightly packed into a rectangular box with internal dimensions 12.0cm × 10.0cm × 6.0cm. Each cube is either solid concrete or solid steel. There are twice as many steel cubes as concrete cubes. What is the total mass of the cubes in the box? (density of concrete = 2.0 g/cm³; density of steel = 8.0 g/cm³)

steps.title: Since steel concrete concrete cubes steel cubes
steps.content: Since steel:concrete $=2:1$, concrete $=30$ cubes, steel $=60$ cubes.

subtopic: wave speed from timing of successive peaks
question: P and Q are two fixed points on the surface of the ocean which are 6.0m apart. An ocean wave travels in the direction P to Q. The wave has a frequency of 0.50Hz and travels at a constant speed. A wave peak passes Q at time t=0s. The next wave peak travelling towards Q passes P at time t=0.80s. What is the speed of the wave?

steps.title: The peak that passes P at s is
steps.content: The peak that passes P at $t=0.80$s is the one that will arrive at Q one full period after the peak that passed Q at $t=0$, i.e. at $t=2.0$s.

steps.title: So this peak travels from P to Q
steps.content: So this peak travels from P to Q (distance 6.0m) in time $2.0-0.80=1.2$s.

question: A sample contains only one radioactive isotope. This isotope decays in a single step with a half-life of 120 minutes to a stable isotope. The sample is placed near a radiation detector which measures the count rate. The count rate reading is 910 counts per minute (cpm). After 240 minutes the measurement is repeated: the count rate reading is now 238 cpm. After a further 360 minutes have elapsed, a third measurement is made. What is the count rate due to background radiation and what is the expected reading in the third measurement?

steps.title: Third measurement is at minutes half lives cpm
steps.content: Third measurement is at $240+360=600$ minutes $=5$ half-lives: $\frac{896}{32}+14=28+14=42$cpm.

subtopic: terminal velocity with drag and upthrust
question: A solid uniform sphere is made of metal of density $\rho_S$ and has radius $r$ and volume $V$. It falls vertically through a viscous liquid of density $\rho_L$. Three forces act on it: its weight, a drag force $D$ and an upthrust $U$ (equal to the weight of liquid displaced). The drag force is given by $D=krv$, where $v$ is the sphere's speed and $k$ is a constant. What is the terminal speed of the metal sphere?

steps.title: At terminal velocity weight upthrust drag
steps.content: At terminal velocity, weight = upthrust + drag: $\rho_S Vg=\rho_L Vg+krv$.

question: A parachutist of mass 80.0kg drops from a plane travelling at 40.0 m/s, 2000m above the Earth's surface. The parachutist hits the ground at a speed of 5.00 m/s. How much work is done by the parachutist against drag forces during the fall? (Take Earth's gravitational field strength to be 10.0 N/kg.)

subtopic: elastic potential energy and gravitational PE
question: A light spring of unstretched length 0.10m has a spring constant of 20 N/m. The spring is suspended vertically and a load of mass 0.050kg is attached to its end. The load is pulled vertically downwards until the length of the spring is 0.30m, then released. What is the speed of the load at the instant the spring returns to its unstretched length? (gravitational field strength = 10 N/kg; assume resistive forces are negligible)

steps.title: As the load rises m to reach natural
steps.content: As the load rises 0.20m to reach natural length, it gains GPE $=mgh=0.050(10)(0.20)=0.1$J.

steps.title: By energy conservation KE at natural length J
steps.content: By energy conservation, KE at natural length $=0.4-0.1=0.3$J.

question: A rocket travelling in space is burning its fuel at a constant rate. By expelling the burnt fuel through a nozzle, the engine is applying a constant force to the rocket. What is happening to the magnitude of the acceleration of the rocket?

options.A: It is increasing at an increasing rate.
options.B: It is increasing at a constant rate.
options.C: It is increasing at a decreasing rate.
options.E: It is decreasing at an increasing rate.
options.F: It is decreasing at a constant rate.
options.G: It is decreasing at a decreasing rate.

steps.title: Acceleration where is constant and decreases linearly with
steps.content: Acceleration $a=F/m$ where $F$ is constant and $m$ decreases linearly with time as fuel burns at a constant rate.

steps.title: Since and decreases linearly toward zero is a
steps.content: Since $a=F/m(t)$ and $m(t)$ decreases linearly toward zero, $a$ is a convex (reciprocal-shaped) function of time, so it increases at an increasing rate.

question:
Consider only the first three metals in Group 1 (Li, Na, K) and only the first three elements in Group 17 (F, Cl, Br). Which of the following statements is/are correct for the compound lithium bromide?
1. It is formed from the least reactive of the three Group 17 elements.
2. It is formed from the least reactive of the three Group 1 elements and the Group 17 element (of the three) with the lowest boiling point.
3. It is formed from the Group 1 element (of the three) with the highest melting point.

steps.title: Group reactivity decreases down the group F Cl
steps.content: Group 17 reactivity decreases down the group (F>Cl>Br), so Br is least reactive — statement 1 is TRUE.

steps.title: Statement is FALSE F has the lowest boiling
steps.content: Statement 2 is FALSE: F has the lowest boiling point of the three halogens, but LiBr uses Br, not F.

steps.title: Group melting points decrease down the group so
steps.content: Group 1 melting points decrease down the group, so Li has the highest melting point — statement 3 is TRUE (and Li is also least reactive of Li, Na, K, consistent with LiBr).

question:
Use the following data:

| gas | melting point / °C | boiling point / °C |
|---|---|---|
| hydrogen | -259 | -253 |
| nitrogen | -210 | -196 |
| oxygen | -219 | -183 |
| neon | -249 | -246 |
| argon | -189 | -186 |

Water and carbon dioxide were removed from a sample of air and the remaining mixture was cooled to -260°C. The three most abundant remaining elements are to be separated by fractional distillation. In which order would these three elements be collected?

steps.title: The three most abundant elements in air are
steps.content: The three most abundant elements in air are nitrogen (~78%), oxygen (~21%) and argon (~0.9%).

steps.title: In fractional distillation the gas with the lowest
steps.content: In fractional distillation, the gas with the lowest boiling point boils off and is collected first: nitrogen (-196^\circ\mathrm{C}), then argon (-186^\circ\mathrm{C}), then oxygen (-183^\circ\mathrm{C}).

subtopic: determining molecular formula from an alkene
question:
Molecule J is a straight-chain hydrocarbon containing one carbon-carbon double bond. The relative atomic mass (Ar) of hydrogen is 1 and carbon is 12. What is the minimum additional information needed to determine the molecular formula of molecule J?
1. The percentage by mass of carbon in the molecule.
2. The percentage by mass of hydrogen in the molecule.
3. The relative molar mass (Mr) of the molecule.

steps.title: An alkene has general formula so the ratio
steps.content: An alkene has general formula $C_nH_{2n}$, so the ratio of C to H by mass is always $\frac{12n}{2n}=6:1$ regardless of $n$ — percentage composition alone (statements 1 or 2) gives no new information.

steps.title: Only the molar mass can be used to
steps.content: Only the molar mass $M_r=14n$ can be used to solve for $n$ and determine the molecular formula.

subtopic: identifying white solids by qualitative tests
question:
Which of the following tests could be used, on its own, to distinguish between all three of the following white solids: potassium carbonate, calcium chloride and sodium sulfate?
1. Add a small amount of each solid separately to a platinum wire and hold in a colourless flame.
2. Dissolve a small amount of each solid separately in deionised water and add a few drops of sodium hydroxide solution.
3. Dissolve a small amount of each solid separately in deionised water and add a few drops of hydrochloric acid, followed by barium chloride solution.

steps.title: Test flame test K gives lilac Ca gives
steps.content: Test 1 (flame test): K$^+$ gives lilac, Ca$^{2+}$ gives brick-red, Na$^+$ gives yellow — all three distinguishable.

steps.title: Test NaOH only Ca gives a white precipitate
steps.content: Test 2 (NaOH): only Ca$^{2+}$ gives a white precipitate; K$_2$CO$_3$ and Na$_2$SO$_4$ give no visible reaction — cannot distinguish these two.

steps.title: Test HCl causes K CO to fizz CO
steps.content: Test 3: HCl causes K$_2$CO$_3$ to fizz (CO$_2$ released); adding BaCl$_2$ afterward gives a white precipitate with sulfate (Na$_2$SO$_4$) but not with CaCl$_2$ — all three distinguishable.

question: Which one of the following formulae is correct for the compound given?

steps.title: Ammonium ion is NH and carbonate is CO
steps.content: Ammonium ion is NH$_4^+$ and carbonate is CO$_3^{2-}$, so two ammonium ions balance one carbonate: $(NH_4)_2CO_3$ is correct.

steps.title: The others are wrong Al SO Ca OH
steps.content: (The others are wrong: Al$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$, Ca(OH)$_2$, Mg(NO$_3$)$_2$, KBr.)

question: Iodic acid, HIO3, can be made from iodine in the following reaction: $I_2+wH_2O+xCl_2\to yHIO_3+zHCl$. What is the value of $x$ when the equation is balanced?

Some dilute aqueous solutions were electrolysed using graphite electrodes. Which of the rows shows the correct products of electrolysis?

| | aqueous electrolyte | cathode product | anode product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | potassium hydroxide | potassium | oxygen |
| 2 | copper(II) chloride | chlorine | copper |
| 3 | sodium sulfate | hydrogen | sulfur |

steps.title: Row potassium is too reactive to be discharged
steps.content: Row 1: potassium is too reactive to be discharged; hydrogen forms at the cathode instead — wrong.

steps.title: Row copper less reactive than hydrogen forms at
steps.content: Row 2: copper (less reactive than hydrogen) forms at the cathode and chlorine at the anode — the row has cathode/anode reversed, so wrong.

steps.title: Row hydrogen correctly forms at the cathode Na
steps.content: Row 3: hydrogen correctly forms at the cathode (Na too reactive), but at the anode oxygen forms, not sulfur (sulfate ion is too stable to discharge) — wrong.

steps.title: None of the rows are fully correct
steps.content: None of the rows are fully correct.

subtopic: periodic table trends across periods and groups
question:
Which of the following statements about elements in the Periodic Table is/are correct?
1. When the element in Period 5, Group 2 reacts with the element in Period 3, Group 17, a redox reaction occurs.
2. In each Group, the elements from Period 2 are more reactive than the elements from Period 5.
3. The compound formed between the element in Period 2, Group 14 and the element in Period 3, Group 17 will have a simple molecular structure.

steps.title: Statement Period Group Sr reacting with Period Group
steps.content: Statement 1: Period 5 Group 2 (Sr) reacting with Period 3 Group 17 (Cl) forms SrCl$_2$ via electron transfer, which is a redox reaction — TRUE.

steps.title: Statement reactivity trends differ by group e g
steps.content: Statement 2: reactivity trends differ by group (e.g.

steps.title: Group reactivity increases down the group so Period
steps.content: Group 1 reactivity increases down the group, so Period 2's Li is less reactive than Period 5's Rb) — FALSE in general.

steps.title: Statement Period Group carbon with Period Group Cl
steps.content: Statement 3: Period 2 Group 14 (carbon) with Period 3 Group 17 (Cl) forms CCl$_4$, a simple covalent molecular compound — TRUE.

question:
Which of the following equations represent(s) a redox reaction?
1. $K_2Cr_2O_7+2KOH\to2K_2CrO_4+H_2O$
2. $8HNO_3+3C_2H_6O+K_2Cr_2O_7\to2KNO_3+3C_2H_4O+7H_2O+2Cr(NO_3)_3$
3. $H_2O+SO_2\to H_2SO_3$

steps.title: Reaction chromium remains in the oxidation state throughout
steps.content: Reaction 1: chromium remains in the +6 oxidation state throughout (Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}\to$ CrO$_4^{2-}$ is not a redox change in oxidation number) — not redox.

steps.title: Reaction chromium is reduced from in Cr O
steps.content: Reaction 2: chromium is reduced from +6 (in Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$) to +3 (in Cr(NO$_3$)$_3$) while the alcohol is oxidised to an aldehyde — this is redox.

steps.title: Reaction no oxidation states change simple addition reaction
steps.content: Reaction 3: no oxidation states change (simple addition reaction) — not redox.

question: A paper chromatogram is set up with an orange food colouring spotted on the baseline. Ten minutes after the start, the solvent front has moved 15.0cm up the paper from the baseline and a yellow spot is 12.0cm above the baseline. Five minutes later, the solvent front has moved up a further 10.0cm. How far from the baseline will the yellow spot be 15 minutes after the start?

steps.title: Constant for a given substance solvent
steps.content: $R_f=\frac{\text{distance travelled by spot}}{\text{distance travelled by solvent front}}=\frac{12.0}{15.0}=0.8$ (constant for a given substance/solvent).

steps.title: At minutes the solvent front is at cm
steps.content: At 15 minutes, the solvent front is at $15.0+10.0=25.0$cm.

subtopic: titration to find mass in a mixture
question: 10g of a mixture of solid magnesium hydroxide, Mg(OH)$_2$, and solid sodium hydroxide, NaOH, is added to an excess of water and stirred. One component dissolves; the other (assumed completely insoluble) is filtered off. 50cm³ of 1.0 mol/dm³ sulfuric acid exactly neutralises the remaining solution. What is the mass of magnesium hydroxide in the original mixture? (Mr: Mg(OH)$_2$ = 58; NaOH = 40)

steps.title: Mg OH is insoluble and filtered off so
steps.content: Mg(OH)$_2$ is insoluble and filtered off, so only NaOH is neutralised.

steps.title: Moles mol so moles NaOH mol mass g
steps.content: Moles $H_2SO_4=0.050\times1.0=0.05$mol. $2NaOH+H_2SO_4\to Na_2SO_4+2H_2O$, so moles NaOH $=0.1$mol, mass $=0.1\times40=4.0$g.

question: 100cm³ of ethane is mixed with 1400cm³ of oxygen and the mixture is ignited. All volumes are measured at atmospheric pressure and 150°C. What will be the total volume of gas after complete combustion? (Assume equal amounts of any gas at the same temperature and pressure occupy the same volume.)

steps.title: Cm ethane requires cm O excess O remaining
steps.content: $2C_2H_6+7O_2\to4CO_2+6H_2O$. 100cm^3 ethane requires $\frac{7}{2}\times100=350$cm^3 O$_2$; excess O$_2$ remaining $=1400-350=1050$cm^3.

steps.title: Products CO cm H O gas at C

subtopic: strong vs weak acids and neutralisation stoichiometry
question:
Hydrochloric acid, sulfuric acid and phosphoric(V) acid are inorganic acids. Phosphoric(V) acid ionises in a series of equilibria: $H_3PO_4\rightleftharpoons H^++H_2PO_4^-$, $H_2PO_4^-\rightleftharpoons H^++HPO_4^{2-}$, $HPO_4^{2-}\rightleftharpoons H^++PO_4^{3-}$. 0.1 mol/dm³ hydrochloric acid has a pH of 1.0 at room temperature. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The pH of 0.1 mol/dm³ sulfuric acid is greater than 1.0 at room temperature.
2. H$_2$PO$_4^-$ can act as an acid or as a base.
3. 30cm³ of calcium hydroxide solution exactly neutralises 20cm³ of phosphoric(V) acid solution when both solutions are the same concentration.

steps.title: Statement sulfuric acid is diprotic so mol dm
steps.content: Statement 1: sulfuric acid is diprotic, so 0.1mol/dm^3 H$_2$SO$_4$ produces more H$^+$ than 0.1mol/dm^3 HCl, giving a LOWER (not greater) pH than 1.0 — FALSE.

steps.title: Statement H PO can donate a proton act
steps.content: Statement 2: H$_2$PO$_4^-$ can donate a proton (act as acid, forming HPO$_4^{2-}$) or accept one (act as base, reforming H$_3$PO$_4$) — TRUE (amphoteric).

steps.title: Statement requires a mole ratio of Ca OH
steps.content: Statement 3: $3Ca(OH)_2+2H_3PO_4\to Ca_3(PO_4)_2+6H_2O$ requires a 3:2 mole ratio of Ca(OH)$_2$:H$_3$PO$_4$; for equal concentrations, 30cm^3:20cm^3 = 3:2, matching exactly — TRUE.

subtopic: reaction rate graphs and limiting reagent
question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph of volume of CO2 formed vs time, with line P representing the first reaction, and lines A-F showing alternative possible curves for the second reaction.]
Calcium carbonate reacts with hydrochloric acid: $CaCO_3(s)+2HCl(aq)\to CaCl_2(aq)+H_2O(l)+CO_2(g)$. Line P shows how the volume of CO$_2$ formed changes with time when 4.0g of calcium carbonate reacts with 50cm³ of 1.0 mol/dm³ hydrochloric acid at 20°C. A second reaction is carried out under identical conditions with the same mass of calcium carbonate but using 50cm³ of 2.0 mol/dm³ hydrochloric acid. Which line best represents the second reaction? (Mr: CaCO3 = 100)

steps.title: Reaction P moles CaCO mol moles HCl x
steps.content: Reaction 1 (P): moles CaCO3=4.0/100=0.04mol; moles HCl=50x1.0/1000=0.05mol; required HCl for all CaCO3=0.08mol>0.05mol available, so HCl is limiting -> moles CO2=0.05/2=0.025mol.

steps.title: Reaction double concentration mol dm moles HCl x
steps.content: Reaction 2 (double concentration, 2.0mol/dm3): moles HCl=50x2.0/1000=0.10mol>0.08mol needed, so CaCO3 becomes limiting -> moles CO2=0.04mol (matches moles CaCO3, 1:1 ratio) -- both a HIGHER final volume than P (0.04 vs 0.025mol) AND a FASTER initial rate (higher acid concentration).

subtopic: isotope abundance from relative atomic mass
question: An element Z forms an ionic compound ZSO$_4$ with $M_r=120.4$. The ion of Z in ZSO$_4$ has 10 electrons. Element Z has three isotopes L, M, N with 12, 13 and 14 neutrons respectively. The percentage abundances of isotopes M and N are the same. What is the percentage abundance of isotope L in element Z in ZSO$_4$? ($M_r$ of SO$_4^{2-}$ = 96.1)

steps.title: Atomic mass of Z matching magnesium
steps.content: Atomic mass of Z $=120.4-96.1=24.3$, matching magnesium.

steps.title: Z has electrons so Z has protons Mg
steps.content: Z$^{2+}$ has 10 electrons, so Z has 12 protons (Mg).

steps.title: Isotopes L M N have mass numbers
steps.content: Isotopes L, M, N have mass numbers 24, 25, 26.

steps.title: Let abundance of M N so L
steps.content: Let abundance of M = N = $x\%$, so L $=100-2x\%$.

question: Complete combustion of 1 mol of hydrocarbon X requires exactly 8.5 mol of oxygen. Incomplete combustion of 1 mol of hydrocarbon X, to form carbon monoxide and water only, requires exactly 5.5 mol of oxygen. How many hydrogen atoms are there in one molecule of hydrocarbon X?

subtopic: molar enthalpy of neutralisation from graph
question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph of maximum temperature vs volume of acid/alkali mixed, with two extrapolated straight lines crossing at the maximum temperature rise point.]
Four separate experiments were carried out using different quantities of 2 mol/dm³ hydrochloric acid and 2 mol/dm³ sodium hydroxide in insulated polystyrene cups. The maximum temperature was recorded and plotted, along with the temperatures of acid and alkali alone; two straight lines were extrapolated to find the maximum temperature rise. What is the molar enthalpy change for the neutralisation reaction, in kJ/mol? (specific heat capacity of solutions = 4 J g$^{-1}$ °C$^{-1}$; density of solutions = 1 g/cm³; assume all heat is transferred to the solution)

steps.title: Reading the ex mol of HCl and NaOH
steps.content: Reading the ex05mol of HCl and NaOH reacting.

solution.trap: trapolated graph, a typical setup uses 25cm^3+25cm^3=50cm^3 (mass 50g) with a temperature rise of about 15^\circ\mathrm{C} at the maximum point, giving 0.

subtopic: group 1 metals reacting with alcohol - mass change
question: An experiment is carried out using the first three metals in Group 1: lithium, sodium and potassium. The initial masses of three open beakers, each containing 100g samples of an alcohol, are recorded. Equal small masses of lithium, sodium and potassium are separately added to the three beakers on electronic balances. Each metal reacts similarly, releasing hydrogen gas, and after the reaction is complete the final mass of each beaker and its contents is recorded and compared with the initial mass. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: The beaker with lithium added would decrease in mass the most.
options.B: The beaker with sodium added would decrease in mass the most.
options.C: The beaker with potassium added would decrease in mass the most.
options.D: All three beakers would show the same decrease in mass.
options.E: The beaker with lithium added would increase in mass the most.
options.F: The beaker with sodium added would increase in mass the most.
options.G: The beaker with potassium added would increase in mass the most.
options.H: All three beakers would show the same increase in mass.

steps.title: Net mass change mass of metal added mass
steps.content: Net mass change $=$ mass of metal added $-$ mass of H$_2$ released.

steps.title: For a mass of metal with atomic mass
steps.content: For a mass $m$ of metal with atomic mass $M$, moles metal $=m/M$, and moles H$_2$ released $=\frac{1}{2}(m/M)$, so mass of H$_2=\frac{m}{M}$ (since $M(H_2)=2$).

steps.title: Since is fixed and increases from Li to
steps.content: Since $m$ is fixed and $M$ increases from Li (7) to Na (23) to K (39), the factor $\left(1-\frac{1}{M}\right)$ increases, so potassium gives the greatest mass increase.

question: X is a solution of sulfuric acid. 20.0cm³ of X is diluted with distilled water to produce 500cm³ of solution Y. 10.0cm³ of Y is exactly neutralised by 40.0cm³ of 0.0500 mol/dm³ aqueous potassium hydroxide. What is the concentration of sulfuric acid in X?

steps.title: Moles KOH mol so moles in cm of
Dilution factor from X to Y
steps.content: Dilution factor from X to Y $=500/20=25$.

question: An electric current is the flow of charged particles. In the electrolysis of aluminium oxide using inert electrodes, the current flows at $5.00\times10^{-6}$ moles of electrons per second. Assume only aluminium oxide is present and the aluminium is a single isotope $^{27}_{13}$Al. What mass of aluminium is produced in 48 seconds?

steps.title: Moles of electrons mol so moles Al mol
steps.content: Moles of electrons $=5.00\times10^{-6}\times48=2.4\times10^{-4}$mol. $Al^{3+}+3e^-\to Al$, so moles Al $=\frac{2.4\times10^{-4}}{3}=8.0\times10^{-5}$mol.

question:
Which of the following cells do not contain mitochondria?
1. bacterial cells
2. embryonic stem cells
3. mature red blood cells
4. potato cells

steps.title: Bacterial cells are prokaryotic and lack mitochondria and
steps.content: Bacterial cells are prokaryotic and lack mitochondria (and all membrane-bound organelles).

steps.title: Mature mammalian red blood cells lose their nucleus
steps.content: Mature mammalian red blood cells lose their nucleus and organelles, including mitochondria, during development.

steps.title: Embryonic stem cells and potato cells are eukaryotic
steps.content: Embryonic stem cells and potato cells are eukaryotic and contain mitochondria.

question:
Which of the following could lead to phenotypic variation between a father and his son?
1. time spent in sunlight
2. their genomes
3. their diets

steps.title: Phenotypic variation arises from both genetic differences
steps.content: Phenotypic variation arises from both genetic differences and environmental factors.

steps.title: Time in sunlight e g tanning and diet
steps.content: Time in sunlight (e.g. tanning) and diet are environmental factors affecting phenotype; the father and son also have different genomes (son inherits only half from father, plus alleles from mother).

question:
[DIAGRAM: A diagram of part of the carbon cycle showing four labelled boxes P, Q, R, S connected by arrows representing processes such as photosynthesis, respiration, feeding, and decomposition.]
The diagram shows part of the carbon cycle. Which row identifies the correct descriptions for boxes P, Q, R and S (CO2 in atmosphere; carbon-rich compounds in animals; carbon-rich compounds in decomposers; carbon-rich compounds in plants)?

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Using the standard carbon cycle diagram CO in
steps.content: Using the standard carbon cycle diagram (CO2 in atmosphere is fixed by plants via photosynthesis; plants are eaten by animals; both animals and plants are broken down by decomposers, which respire CO2 back to the atmosphere), the boxes are matched to their descriptions as per the diagram's arrow directions, giving the arrangement Q, S, P, R.

question:
A section of double-stranded DNA contains 4500 base pairs. 10% of the bases present are adenine. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. There are 450 thymine bases present.
2. 40% of the bases present are cytosine.
3. There are 3600 guanine bases present.

steps.title: By base pairing thymine adenine not so statement
steps.content: By base pairing, thymine $=$ adenine $=900$ (not 450, so statement 1 is FALSE).

steps.title: Remaining bases split equally between C and G
steps.content: Remaining bases: $9000-1800=7200$ split equally between C and G $=3600$ each.

steps.title: Cytosine statement TRUE and guanine statement TRUE

question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph of percentage of maximum enzyme activity vs pH (1-11), with one curve for pepsin peaking at low pH (~pH 2) and another for trypsin peaking at higher pH (~pH 8).]
Pepsin and trypsin are both protease enzymes found in the human digestive system. In the human digestive system:
1. pepsin is most active in the stomach.
2. trypsin would be inactive in the stomach.
3. pepsin could be a substrate for trypsin.
4. pepsin is most active at low acidity and trypsin most active at high acidity.
Which statements are correct?

steps.title: From the graph pepsin s activity peaks around
steps.content: From the graph, pepsin's activity peaks around pH 2 (the acidic stomach environment) — statement 1 TRUE.

steps.title: Trypsin s activity is near zero at low
steps.content: Trypsin's activity is near zero at low (stomach) pH — statement 2 TRUE.

steps.title: Pepsin is itself a protein so it can
steps.content: Pepsin is itself a protein, so it can be digested (acted upon) as a substrate by another protease like trypsin — statement 3 TRUE.

solution.trap: incorrectly: pepsin is most active at HIGH acidity (low pH), not low acidity, so statement 4 is FALSE.

subtopic: chromosome numbers after non-disjunction and mitosis
question:
[DIAGRAM: Two human gametes, cell P and cell Q, fusing to form cell R; cell P carries an additional copy of one chromosome so it has one more chromosome than cell Q. Cell R divides to form two cells S and T, which grow into two separate individuals.]
Using this information, which row shows the correct number of chromosomes in the nucleus of cell R and in the nucleus of cell T?

steps.title: Cell P has one extra cell Q is
steps.content: Cell P has one extra (24), cell Q is normal (23).

steps.title: Cell R divides by mitosis producing two genetically
steps.content: Cell R divides by mitosis (producing two genetically identical individuals, i.e. twins), which preserves chromosome number, so cell T also has 47 chromosomes.

question:
SCID is an inherited condition in humans. In one type of SCID, white blood cells cannot make the functional enzyme ADA, needed for these cells to divide by mitosis during an immune response. Gene therapy inserts a gene into DNA of bone marrow stem cells from a person with this condition; these cells are returned to the body and can divide and differentiate into white blood cells, increasing white blood cell numbers if successful. Which of the following statements correctly describe(s) how this method of gene therapy might work?
1. Genetically altered stem cells differentiate into white blood cells able to produce functional ADA.
2. Genetically altered stem cells can differentiate into gametes so that offspring will not inherit SCID.
3. The sequence of bases in the DNA of the white blood cells, derived from the genetically altered stem cells, has changed.

steps.title: Statement is TRUE this is the described mechanism
steps.content: Statement 1 is TRUE: this is the described mechanism of the therapy.

steps.title: Statement is FALSE bone marrow stem cells here
steps.content: Statement 2 is FALSE: bone marrow stem cells here are somatic (not germline), so they do not differentiate into gametes, and the therapy does not affect what is passed on to offspring.

steps.title: Statement is TRUE inserting a functional gene changes
steps.content: Statement 3 is TRUE: inserting a functional gene changes the DNA base sequence of the treated cells and their white blood cell descendants.

subtopic: identifying organs from blood composition changes
question:
The table shows concentrations of substances in blood entering and leaving three different organs of a person, taken 10 minutes after eating a carbohydrate-rich meal:

| organ | glucose in | O2 in | urea in | glucose out | O2 out | urea out |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 9.0 | 65 | 3.0 | 9.0 | 65 | 3.0 |
| 2 | 9.0 | 85 | 3.0 | 9.0 | 70 | 2.4 |
| 3 | 9.0 | 85 | 3.0 | 12.0 | 70 | 3.0 |

Which row identifies the organs (kidney / small intestine / chambers of the right side of the heart)?

steps.title: Organ shows no change in any value blood
steps.content: Organ 1 shows no change in any value — blood simply passes through without exchange, matching the right side of the heart (which pumps blood without gas/nutrient exchange).

steps.title: Organ shows a drop in oxygen and urea
steps.content: Organ 2 shows a drop in oxygen and urea with unchanged glucose — matching the kidney, which uses O$_2$ for active reabsorption and filters out urea.

steps.title: Organ shows increased glucose with dropped oxygen and
steps.content: Organ 3 shows increased glucose with dropped oxygen and unchanged urea — matching the small intestine, which absorbs glucose from digested food and uses oxygen for active transport.

subtopic: sickle cell trait and malaria selection
question:
The table shows information about sickle cell anaemia and malaria:

| genotype | phenotype | comment |
|---|---|---|
| MM | no sickle cell anaemia | can be infected with malaria |
| Mm | no sickle cell anaemia | shows resistance to malaria |
| mm | shows sickle cell anaemia | shows more resistance to malaria than Mm |

Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. In areas without malaria, human populations are likely to have a low number of people with the m allele.
2. In areas with malaria, only those individuals that are heterozygous will be able to pass on their alleles to the next generation.
3. Presence of malaria has caused a mutation of the M allele to the m allele leading to an increased chance of survival in the heterozygous state.

steps.title: Statement is TRUE without malaria the mm genotype
steps.content: Statement 1 is TRUE: without malaria, the mm genotype has only the disadvantage (sickle cell anaemia) with no compensating benefit, so natural selection reduces the frequency of the m allele.

steps.title: Statement is FALSE MM and mm individuals can
steps.content: Statement 2 is FALSE: MM and mm individuals can also reproduce (mm just has increased risk, not zero survival), so it's not true that ONLY heterozygotes can pass on alleles.

steps.title: Statement is FALSE malaria does not cause the
steps.content: Statement 3 is FALSE: malaria does not cause the mutation to occur — mutations arise randomly; malaria only selects for the m allele where it already exists.

question:
Which of the following could be a result of the addition of a large amount of organic material into a slow flowing river?
1. A decrease in biodiversity in the river.
2. A reduction in the oxygen concentration gradient between the air and the water.
3. An increase in the size of at least one aquatic population.

steps.title: Excess organic material fuels a population explosion of
steps.content: Excess organic material fuels a population explosion of decomposer bacteria, which consume dissolved oxygen (biological oxygen demand), causing oxygen-sensitive species to die off and reducing biodiversity — statement 1 TRUE.

steps.title: As dissolved oxygen in the water falls further
steps.content: As dissolved oxygen in the water falls further below the atmospheric level, the concentration gradient between air and water INCREASES, not decreases — statement 2 FALSE.

steps.title: The decomposer bacteria population itself increases in size
steps.content: The decomposer/bacteria population itself increases in size — statement 3 TRUE.

question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph of mass of product produced vs time for an enzyme-controlled reaction under two conditions X and Y, with curve X reaching a lower final plateau than curve Y, both starting with similar initial rate.]
The graph shows the mass of product produced over time for an enzyme-controlled reaction in two different conditions, X and Y. All other variables were kept constant. Which of the following could be correct for this graph?
1. Condition X is a higher pH than condition Y.
2. Condition X has less substrate supplied than condition Y.
3. Condition X is a lower pH than condition Y.
4. Condition X has more substrate supplied than condition Y.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Given the shape of the graph both conditions
steps.content: Given the shape of the graph (both conditions reach different final plateaus but the difference is consistent with pH affecting enzyme activity/rate rather than substrate amount alone), either a higher or lower pH in X relative to Y could account for the observed pattern depending on which side of the optimum each condition lies, so statements 1 and 3 are both plausible explanations, while the substrate-amount explanations (2 and 4) do not fit the shape shown.

subtopic: active transport, diffusion and osmosis of ions
question:
The table shows the concentration of potassium ions in several locations:

| location | [K+] |
|---|---|
| bacterial cell cytoplasm | 30 mmol/dm³ |
| mammalian blood plasma | 4000 µmol/dm³ |
| mammalian heart cell cytoplasm | 1.0×10² mmol/dm³ |
| sea water | 3.0×10⁴ µmol/dm³ |
| yeast cell cytoplasm | 300 mmol/dm³ |

Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. A mammalian heart cell needs energy from respiration in order to obtain more potassium ions from blood plasma.
2. If a yeast cell is placed in sea water then it will lose potassium ions by osmosis.
3. There is no concentration gradient for potassium ions between a bacterial cell and sea water.

steps.title: Converting units blood plasma sea water
steps.content: Converting units: blood plasma $=4000\mu\text{mol/dm}^3=4\text{mmol/dm}^3$; sea water $=3.0\times10^4\mu\text{mol/dm}^3=30\text{mmol/dm}^3$.

steps.title: Heart cell mmol dm has far more K
steps.content: Heart cell (100mmol/dm^3) has far more K$^+$ than plasma (4mmol/dm^3), so moving more K$^+$ in requires active transport against the gradient — statement 1 TRUE.

steps.title: Statement is FALSE because it confuses ion movement
steps.content: Statement 2 is FALSE because it confuses ion movement with osmosis: osmosis concerns water movement, not K$^+$ movement; K$^+$ moves by diffusion/active transport, not osmosis.

steps.title: Bacterial cytoplasm mmol dm equals sea water mmol
steps.content: Bacterial cytoplasm (30mmol/dm^3) equals sea water (30mmol/dm^3), so there is no concentration gradient for K$^+$ — statement 3 TRUE.

question:
[DIAGRAM: A 100m² area of grassland with scattered black circles representing individual plants, sampled with 10 large (50cm×50cm) quadrats and 10 small (10cm×10cm) quadrats.]
The abundance of a plant species in a habitat can be measured by density (number per m²) or frequency (number of quadrats in which the species occurs). The area is sampled randomly, first using 10 large quadrats and then 10 small quadrats. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The overall density calculated from sampling with either size quadrat will always be the same.
2. The overall density calculated from sampling will always be 1 plant per m².
3. The frequency obtained using the small quadrat will always be lower than that obtained with the large quadrat.

steps.title: All three statements use the absolute word always
steps.content: All three statements use the absolute word 'always', which is too strong: sampling is random so results vary between samples (statement 1 false — different quadrat sizes and random placement give different, not guaranteed identical, density estimates), the exact density value depends on the actual plant distribution rather than being fixed at 1/m^2 (statement 2 false), and while small quadrats often give a lower frequency than large ones, this is not guaranteed in every random sample (statement 3 false).

steps.title: None of the statements are correct
steps.content: None of the statements are correct.

subtopic: measuring rate of photosynthesis via gas bubble
question:
[DIAGRAM: Apparatus with a light source, pondweed submerged in water, and a capillary tube with a gas bubble whose movement along a scale is measured to determine the rate of photosynthetic gas production.]
A student investigated the endothermic reaction of photosynthesis in pondweed using a capillary tube of 2mm diameter. The experiment ran for 5 minutes, and the rate of gas production (calculated from the bubble's movement) is $2\pi\,\text{mm}^3$ per minute. Which row of the table is correct (total distance moved by the bubble; reason the reaction is endothermic; observation if the light source is moved further away)?

options.A: 0.5, releases energy, moves more slowly to the right
options.B: 0.5, takes in energy, moves more slowly to the left
options.C: 2.0, releases energy, moves more slowly to the left
options.D: 2.0, releases energy, moves more slowly to the right
options.E: 2.5, takes in energy, moves more slowly to the left
options.F: 2.5, takes in energy, moves more slowly to the right
options.G: 10.0, releases energy, moves more slowly to the left
options.H: 10.0, takes in energy, moves more slowly to the right

steps.title: Total volume of gas over minutes
steps.content: Total volume of gas over 5 minutes $=2\pi\times5=10\pi\,\text{mm}^3$.

steps.title: Photosynthesis absorbs light energy to drive the reaction
steps.content: Photosynthesis absorbs light energy to drive the reaction, so it is endothermic (takes in energy).

steps.title: Moving the light source further away reduces light
steps.content: Moving the light source further away reduces light intensity, slowing the rate of photosynthesis, so the bubble moves more slowly (in the direction indicated in the diagram, to the right).

question: The placenta is an organ that develops during pregnancy. One function of the placenta is to allow the exchange of gases between the mother and the developing foetus. [DIAGRAM: the maternal and foetal blood flow between P and Q in a section of the placenta of a healthy small mammal; maternal blood flows from P to Q while foetal blood flows from Q to P (opposite/countercurrent directions); the concentration of oxygen in both maternal and foetal blood is measured at regular distances along this section] Which graph illustrates the concentration of oxygen in both maternal and foetal blood between P and Q?

options.A: both maternal and foetal curves increase from P to Q, converging near Q
options.B: both curves decrease from P to Q, with maternal falling faster than foetal so that foetal ends up above maternal, both converging toward a lower plateau near Q
options.C: both curves decrease from P to Q (maternal above foetal throughout, roughly linear, near-parallel)
options.D: maternal decreases steeply then levels; foetal increases from low then levels (concurrent-flow pattern)
options.E: maternal decreases then crosses below foetal, which increases
options.F: maternal decreases along a smooth curve while foetal increases along a smooth curve, the two curves bowing apart in the middle and meeting at Q

steps.title: Maternal blood flows P to Q foetal blood
steps.content: Maternal blood flows P to Q; foetal blood flows Q to P (counter-current, as shown by the opposing arrows).

steps.title: Maternal O is highest at P freshly oxygenated
steps.content: Maternal O2 is highest at P (freshly oxygenated) and falls toward Q as O2 diffuses out.

steps.title: Foetal blood enters at Q deoxygenated and picks
steps.content: Foetal blood enters at Q (deoxygenated) and picks up O2 along its journey to P, so foetal O2 is also highest near P and lowest at Q.

steps.title: True counter current exchange keeps maternal concentratio
steps.content: True counter-current exchange keeps maternal concentration above foetal at every point along the exchange, with both curves declining from P to Q without crossing.

subtopic: cardiac cycle: ventricular volume and ECG
question:
[DIAGRAM: Graph of ventricular volume (cm³, ranging 50-130) and accompanying ECG trace over two consecutive heartbeats, with points X, Y and Z marked at different phases of the cycle.]
The diagram shows the changes in ventricular volume and the accompanying ECG trace during two consecutive heartbeats. Which row is correct about the events happening at X, Y and Z (at X blood is being pumped into; at Y valves between atria and ventricles are; chambers of the heart contracting at Z)?

steps.title: At X ventricular volume is falling rapidly ventricular
steps.content: At X, ventricular volume is falling rapidly (ventricular systole), meaning blood is being ejected into the aorta (and pulmonary artery, but the row specifies aorta).

steps.title: At Y ventricular volume is at its lowest
steps.content: At Y, ventricular volume is at its lowest and about to rise again as ventricles relax and fill, meaning the atrioventricular valves are open, allowing blood to flow from atria to ventricles.

steps.title: At Z the atria are contracting to complete
steps.content: At Z, the atria are contracting to complete ventricular filling just before the next systole.

question:
Three different methods, X, Y and Z, of controlling pathogens in the human body are compared:

| method | affects bacteria | affects viruses | requires cell movement | uses a molecule released from human cells |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X | yes | yes | yes | no |
| Y | yes | no | no | no |
| Z | yes | yes | no | yes |

Which row correctly identifies the methods?

steps.title: Method X requires the movement of human cells
steps.content: Method X requires the movement of human cells and uses no released molecule — this matches phagocytosis (phagocytes move to and engulf pathogens, affecting both bacteria and viruses).

steps.title: Method Y affects only bacteria requires no cell
steps.content: Method Y affects only bacteria, requires no cell movement, and uses no human-derived molecule — this matches taking an antibiotic (an external chemical).

steps.title: Method Z affects both bacteria and viruses without
steps.content: Method Z affects both bacteria and viruses without cell movement, using a molecule released from human cells — this matches antibody production, which can be stimulated by vaccination with inactivated pathogen.

subtopic: osmosis, cell lysis and enzyme access to substrate
question: Catalase is an enzyme found inside plant and animal cells; when catalase reacts with hydrogen peroxide, bubbles of oxygen gas form. Red blood cells were placed into either water or plasma (in the dark); plant cells were placed into either water or 0.5 mol/dm³ sucrose solution (in the dark). Assume hydrogen peroxide and catalase do not cross the intact cell surface membrane. Which row shows the results when hydrogen peroxide was added (✓ = bubbles form, x = no bubbles) for: red blood cells in plasma; plant cells in 0.5 mol/dm³ sucrose; red blood cells in water; plant cells in water?

steps.title: In plasma isotonic and sucrose solution isotonic hypertonic
steps.content: In plasma (isotonic) and sucrose solution (isotonic/hypertonic for the walled plant cell), the red blood cell and plant cell membranes remain intact, so H$_2$O$_2$ cannot reach the internal catalase — no bubbles form for these two.

steps.title: In water hypotonic red blood cells swell and
steps.content: In water (hypotonic), red blood cells swell and lyse (no cell wall to prevent bursting), releasing catalase to react with external H$_2$O$_2$ — bubbles form.

steps.title: Plant cells in water become turgid but the
steps.content: Plant cells in water become turgid but the cell wall prevents lysis, so the membrane stays intact and no bubbles form.

question:
[DIAGRAM: Three family trees (pedigrees) numbered 1, 2, 3, each showing inheritance of a different genetic condition controlled by one gene with one dominant and one recessive allele, with males/females with/without the condition indicated by different symbols.]
In which of the family trees must the male parent be heterozygous? (Assume no new mutations occur and the genes are not on the X chromosome.)

steps.title: A parent must be heterozygous when the pedigree
steps.content: A parent must be heterozygous when the pedigree shows evidence of both alleles being present in that parent — for example, if the parent does not show the recessive condition but has at least one affected offspring (proving they carry a recessive allele) while also not being homozygous recessive themselves.

steps.title: Based on the pattern of affected unaffected offspring
steps.content: Based on the pattern of affected/unaffected offspring shown in family trees 1 and 3, the male parent in these two pedigrees must carry one copy of each allele, i.e. be heterozygous.

subtopic: monohybrid cross proportions across two families
question: In rabbits, the dominant allele B gives black fur colour and the recessive allele b gives brown fur colour. Two male black rabbits of unknown genotype each mated with a different female brown rabbit (bb). What is the expected proportion of brown offspring (i) if both male rabbits are heterozygous, and (ii) if only one male rabbit is heterozygous (the other being homozygous dominant)?

steps.title: If both males are Bb each cross Bb
steps.content: If both males are Bb, each cross Bb\timesbb gives 1:1 Bb:bb, so 0.5 of all offspring (across both families) are brown.

steps.title: If only one male is heterozygous Bb and
steps.content: If only one male is heterozygous (Bb) and the other is homozygous dominant (BB), the Bb\timesbb cross gives 0.5 brown offspring while the BB\timesbb cross gives 0 brown offspring; averaged across both families, the overall proportion of brown offspring is $(0.5+0)/2=0.25$.

question: Simplify fully $5xy^2 \times (5x^2y)^{-3} \times 5x^2y$ where $x$ and $y$ are positive.

steps.title: Combine coefficients and powers separately coefficient
steps.content: Combine coefficients and powers separately: coefficient $= 5 \times 5^{-3} \times 5 = 5^{-1} = 1/5$.

question: Which of the following is a simplification of $2-\dfrac{x+3x^2}{12x^2+x-1}$ where $x > 1$?

subtopic: Changing the subject of a formula
question: Which of the following is a rearrangement of $\dfrac{2}{q} = \dfrac{3p+4}{r}$ so that $q$ is the subject?

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Cross multiply so then rearrange to match the
steps.content: Cross-multiply: $2r = q(3p+4)$, so $q = \dfrac{2r}{3p+4}$, then rearrange to match the given option's form.

question: A circle has its centre at $(0,0)$. What is the equation of the tangent that touches the circle at the point $(4,3)$?

steps.title: The radius to has gradient so the tangent
steps.content: The radius to $(4,3)$ has gradient $3/4$, so the tangent (perpendicular) has gradient $-4/3$.

diagram_skipped: Euclidean tangent-to-circle geometry, not Cartesian circle-line

question: Two solid cylinders, P and Q, are shown, where $x > y$. Cylinder P has diameter $x$ and height $y$. Cylinder Q has diameter $y$ and height $x$. What is the positive difference between the total surface areas of P and Q?

steps.title: Total surface area
steps.content: Total surface area $= 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{2} + \pi d h$.

steps.title: For P d x h y
For Q d y h x

question: Given that $8^x+27^x=\dfrac{13}{36}$ and $8^x-27^x=\dfrac{5}{36}$, what is the value of $x$?

The price of item P is reduced by 10%. The next day, the new price is increased by 10%. The price of item Q is increased by 10%. The next day, the new price is reduced by 10%. How does the final price of each item compare to the original price of that item?

options.A: P: lower than original, Q: lower than original
options.B: P: lower than original, Q: higher than original
options.C: P: higher than original, Q: lower than original
options.D: P: higher than original, Q: higher than original
options.E: P: the same as original, Q: the same as original

steps.title: Both multipliers are equal since multiplication is commut
steps.content: Both multipliers are equal since multiplication is commutative, so both are lower than original.

question:
Here is a pattern of numbers:
Row 1: 1
Row 2: 2 3 4
Row 3: 5 6 7 8 9
Row 4: 10 11 12 13 14 15 16
The pattern of numbers is continued in the same way. What number will appear directly below 196?

steps.title: Since is the last rightmost entry of row
steps.content: Since $14^2=196$, 196 is the last (rightmost) entry of row 14, which starts at $13^2+1=170$.

steps.title: Tracking the triangular layout the entry directly below
steps.content: Tracking the triangular layout, the entry directly below the last number of row 14 is the 14th of the 15 entries in row 15, i.e. $197+26=223$.

question: [DIAGRAM: Triangle SQT is right-angled at Q, with point R on SQ such that SR:RQ = 1:3. Triangle QRP is right-angled at Q. PR = ST = 8 cm, QT = 4 cm.] What is the length of PQ, in cm?

steps.title: In triangle SQT right angle at Q
steps.content: In triangle SQT (right angle at Q): $SQ=\sqrt{ST^2-QT^2}=\sqrt{8^2-4^2}=\sqrt{48}=4\sqrt{3}$.

steps.title: In triangle QRP right angle at Q hypotenuse
steps.content: In triangle QRP (right angle at Q, hypotenuse PR=8): $PQ=\sqrt{PR^2-RQ^2}=\sqrt{64-27}=\sqrt{37}$.

question: Pat and Alex have a combined total of £63. The ratio of Pat's money to Alex's money is 5:2. They each spend an equal amount on sweets. The ratio of Pat's money to Alex's money is now 3:1. How much did Pat spend on sweets?

The curve with equation $y=x^2-4x+5$ meets the straight line with equation $y=2x+c$ at two points, which have $x$-coordinates $p$ and $q$, where $q>p$. Given that $q-p=8$, what is the value of the constant $c$?

An online company sells storage containers: 2 litres (2 available), 3 litres (3 available), 7 litres (4 available), 8 litres (1 available). A customer orders two containers at random from those available. What is the probability that the two containers will have a combined capacity of exactly 10 litres?

steps.title: Total containers total ways to choose
steps.content: Total containers = 10, total ways to choose 2 $=\binom{10}{2}=45$.

question: Given that $y=\dfrac{\sin(-160°)}{\cos 60°}$, what is the value of $y^3$?

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Evaluate using the given trigonometric ratios then cube
steps.content: Evaluate $y$ using the given trigonometric ratios then cube the result.

question: [DIAGRAM: P, Q and R are points on the circumference of a circle with centre O. Angle PQR = 140°, PR = 7 cm.] Which of the following expressions gives the radius of the circle, in cm?

steps.title: Since PQR is a cyclic quadrilateral type angle
steps.content: Since PQR is a cyclic quadrilateral-type angle at the circumference of 140^\circ, the angle subtended by chord PR at the circumference on the major arc is $180^\circ-140^\circ=40^\circ$.

steps.title: By the extended sine rule so
steps.content: By the extended sine rule, $PR = 2R\sin(40^\circ)$, so $R = \dfrac{PR}{2\sin 40^\circ}$.

question: Charlie has a bowl containing red sweets and green sweets only, 9 sweets in total. Charlie eats two sweets from the bowl at random. The probability of Charlie not eating any green sweets is $\dfrac{5}{12}$. What is the probability that Charlie eats two green sweets?

[DIAGRAM: Two right-angled triangles with the same hypotenuse length. One triangle has a 45° angle with adjacent side x cm; the other has a 60° angle with opposite side y cm.] Which of the following is a correct expression for y in terms of x?

The greatest diagonal distance between two vertices of a cuboid is $\sqrt{77}$ cm. A similar cuboid has all its lengths exactly half the lengths of the original cuboid. The sides of this smaller cuboid are 2 cm, 3 cm and $x$ cm. What is the value of $x$, in cm?

steps.title: Since the smaller cuboid s sides are half
steps.content: Since the smaller cuboid's sides are half those of the original, the original sides are $4$, $6$, $2x$.

question: Alex, Cameron and Sam are all taking part in a 400 m race, each running at a different constant speed. Alex is running 12% faster than Cameron, whilst Sam is running 2% slower than Cameron. When Alex crosses the finish line, how many metres is Sam from the finish line?

steps.title: Alex s speed Sam s speed
Distance Sam covers in that time m
steps.content: Distance Sam covers in that time: $0.98v\times\dfrac{400}{1.12v}=\dfrac{0.98}{1.12}\times400=350$ m.

question: A car journey is $m$ miles long. One kilometre is equivalent to $x$ miles. The car uses one litre of fuel to travel a distance of $f$ kilometres. Fuel for the car costs $p$ pence per litre. Which of the following expressions gives the cost of fuel for this journey, in pounds? (There are 100 pence in one pound.)
How many solutions are there to the equation $\tan x = 100x$ where $-360 \le x \le 360$ (x in degrees)?

steps.title: Is a very steep line through the origin
steps.content: $y=100x$ is a very steep line through the origin, so $x=0$ is always a solution.

steps.title: Near each vertical asymptote of at within the
steps.content: Near each vertical asymptote of $\tan x$ (at $\pm90^\circ, \pm270^\circ$ within the range), $\tan x$ sweeps through all real values, so the steep line intersects the curve once very close to each asymptote.

steps.title: This gives at near the four asymptotes solutions
steps.content: This gives $1$ (at $x=0$) $+ 4$ (near the four asymptotes) $= 5$ solutions.
