question: PQR is a triangle. S and T are points on the sides PQ and PR. ST is parallel to QR. PS = 3 cm, ST = $x$ cm, QS = $(x+2)$ cm, QR = $(2x+1)$ cm [DIAGRAM: triangle PQR with ST parallel to QR, S on PQ, T on PR]. What is the length, in cm, of QR?

steps.title: Using similar triangles PST PQR and gives i
steps.content: Using similar triangles PST~PQR: $PQ=PS+SQ=x+5$, and $\dfrac{PS}{PQ}=\dfrac{ST}{QR}$ gives $\dfrac{3}{x+5}=\dfrac{x}{2x+1}$, i.e. $3(2x+1)=x(x+5) \Rightarrow 6x+3=x^2+5x \Rightarrow x^2-x-3=0$.

subtopic: Probability of forming a right triangle
question: Three different numbers are chosen at random from 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. What is the probability that the three numbers form the three sides of a right-angled triangle?

steps.title: There are ways to choose numbers from
steps.content: There are $\binom{5}{3}=10$ ways to choose 3 numbers from 5.

steps.title: Only is a Pythagorean triple since

question: P, Q and R are regular polygons. Q has three times as many sides as P. An interior angle of Q is 10° larger than an interior angle of P. R has twice as many sides as Q. How much larger is an interior angle of R than an interior angle of Q, in degrees?

steps.title: Let P have sides Interior angle formula
steps.content: Let P have $n$ sides. Interior angle formula: $180-\dfrac{360}{n}$.

steps.title: Q has sides R has sides
steps.content: Q has $3n$ sides, R has $6n$ sides.

steps.title: So P has Q has R has sides
steps.content: So P has 24, Q has 72, R has 144 sides.

question: The point $(-1,5)$ is translated to the point $(3,2)$ by two successive translations. The first translation is by the vector $\binom{3p}{-4p}$. The second translation is by the vector $\binom{q}{-2q}$. What is the value of $p+q$?

steps.title: From the first substituting into the second gives
steps.content: From the first, $q=4-3p$; substituting into the second gives $-4p-2(4-3p)=-3 \Rightarrow -4p-8+6p=-3 \Rightarrow 2p=5 \Rightarrow p=2.5$, so $q=4-7.5=-3.5$.

question: Consider the graphs of the form $y=x^2+2ax+a$. What is the complete range of values of $a$ for which the minimum point of the graph lies above the x-axis?

steps.title: For this to be above the x axis
steps.content: For this to be above the x-axis: $a-a^2>0 \Rightarrow a(1-a)>0 \Rightarrow 0<a<1$.

question: There is a constant current in a conducting wire. A charge of 20 C passes through the wire in 1.5 minutes. An 18 cm straight section of this wire lies in a uniform magnetic field, perpendicular to the field direction. The magnetic field strength is 0.15 T. What is the magnitude of the magnetic force on this section of wire?

A rider on a rollercoaster moves very quickly towards a solid wall. While moving, the rider shouts, and hears an echo of the shout from the wall. The echo is quieter than the original shout. How do the amplitude and frequency of the echo heard by the rider compare to the amplitude and frequency of the original shout?

steps.title: The problem states the echo is quieter so
steps.content: The problem states the echo is quieter, so amplitude is lower (energy absorbed/spread by reflection).

steps.title: Since the rider is moving toward the wall
steps.content: Since the rider is moving toward the wall (both effectively source and observer approaching the reflecting surface), the Doppler effect causes a double blue-shift, increasing the frequency of the reflected sound heard by the rider.

question: A system has two large copper tanks of water connected by a solid cylindrical copper bar. Water in tank 1 is at temperature T1, tank 2 at a higher temperature T2 (T1 < T2 at all times). Consider these changes made independently: (1) increase T1, (2) increase T2, (3) increase the length of the copper bar, (4) increase the diameter of the copper bar. Which two changes each independently result in an increase in the rate of conduction of thermal energy along the copper bar?

steps.title: Increasing T reduces decreases rate wrong
steps.content: Increasing T1 reduces $\Delta T$ (decreases rate) — wrong.

steps.title: Increasing T increases increases rate correct
steps.content: Increasing T2 increases $\Delta T$ (increases rate) — correct.

steps.title: Increasing length L decreases rate wrong
steps.content: Increasing length L decreases rate — wrong.

steps.title: Increasing diameter increases cross sectional area A incr
steps.content: Increasing diameter increases cross-sectional area A (increases rate) — correct.

question: Two identical resistors are connected in parallel to a 6.0 V battery. The two resistors dissipate a total power of 0.15 W. One of these resistors is removed from the circuit and connected to a 12 V battery. How much charge passes through this resistor in 6.0 minutes?

steps.title: Each resistor in parallel has the full V
steps.content: Each resistor in parallel has the full 6.0 V across it.

question: A small piece of space debris of mass 0.10 g strikes the International Space Station at a relative speed of 15 000 ms⁻¹. The piece of debris comes to rest relative to the space station in 0.010 s. What is the average force exerted on the space station by the piece of debris during this time?

A block of mass 6.0 kg is pushed along a rough horizontal surface by a constant force of 8.0 N. The block accelerates uniformly from rest. After 4.0 s its velocity is 2.0 ms⁻¹. How much work is done against resistive forces during this 4.0 s?

Ultrasound is used to find a crack inside a cuboid block of metal. A probe on the top surface sends a pulse at t=0 ms, which reflects from both the crack and the bottom surface. [DIAGRAM: graph of reflected pulse strength vs time, showing two peaks between 0 and 0.3 ms]. The speed of ultrasound in the metal is 5000 ms⁻¹. What is the distance between the bottom surface of the metal block and the crack?

steps.title: Reading the graph the two reflected pulses appear
steps.content: Reading the graph, the two reflected pulses appear at approximately $t=0.12$ms and $t=0.20$ms, so $\Delta t\approx0.08$ms $=8\times10^{-5}$s.

question: Power is supplied to an electric motor at 0.800 kW. The motor has an efficiency of 60% and is switched on for half an hour. How much energy is wasted during this time?

steps.title: Useful power W wasted power W
steps.content: Useful power $=0.60\times800=480$ W, wasted power $=320$ W.

question: A circuit includes two ammeters and a resistor R in parallel branches, one branch containing a 3.0 Ω resistor. One ammeter reads 3.3 A (total current) and the other reads 1.8 A (current through R). What is the resistance of resistor R?

steps.title: Current through branch A so voltage across parallel
steps.content: Current through 3.0 Ω branch $=3.3-1.8=1.5$ A, so voltage across parallel section $=1.5\times3.0=4.5$ V.

steps.title: This is also the voltage across R so
steps.content: This is also the voltage across R, so $R=\dfrac{4.5}{1.8}=2.5\,\Omega$.

question: A graph shows potential difference plotted against current for a filament lamp and a resistor [DIAGRAM: V-I graph, lamp curve non-linear, resistor line linear]. The lamp and resistor are connected in parallel to a 6.0 V supply, and the current in the lamp, I, is recorded. In a second circuit, the lamp and resistor are connected in series to the same supply, and the current in the resistor is 0.18 A. The potential difference across the lamp, V, is recorded. What are the values of I in the first circuit and V in the second circuit?

steps.title: From the V one graph at V V
steps.content: From the V-one graph: at V=6.0 V (parallel case, full supply voltage across lamp), read off the lamp current one=0.35 A.

steps.title: In the series case the resistor carries A
steps.content: In the series case, the resistor carries 0.18 A; since the same current flows through the lamp, use the lamp curve to find the voltage across the lamp at one=0.18 A, which reads V=4.4 V (with 1.6V across the resistor, summing to 6.0V total).

subtopic: Impulse in a bounce (ball and bat)
question: A child is bouncing a ball of mass 0.16 kg vertically up and down on a bat. Each time the ball hits the bat the duration of contact is 0.20 s. The speed of the ball immediately before hitting the bat and immediately after it loses contact is 4.0 ms⁻¹. What is the average contact force between the bat and the ball during each collision? (g = 10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: Change in momentum kg ms
steps.content: Change in momentum $\Delta p=m v-(-mv)=2mv=2\times0.16\times4.0=1.28$ kg ms⁻¹.

steps.title: This net force is contact force weight so
steps.content: This net force is (contact force $-$ weight): $F_{net}=F_{contact}-mg$, so $F_{contact}=6.4+(0.16\times10)=6.4+1.6=8.0$ N.

question: A transverse wave on a string has a speed of 500 ms⁻¹. The horizontal distance between two points P and Q on the wave is 4.0 m [DIAGRAM: wave snapshot showing points P, X, Q along the string, X at maximum displacement 6.0mm]. At t=0 ms, point X on the string is at its maximum displacement of 6.0 mm above equilibrium. What is the displacement of point X at t=7.0 ms?

steps.title: The diagram shows TWO complete wave cycles between
steps.content: The diagram shows TWO complete wave cycles between P and Q across the 4.0m span (not one), so $\lambda=4.0/2=2.0$m.

steps.title: At ms the phase is cycles cycle from
steps.content: At $t=7$ms, the phase is $7/4=1.75$ cycles $=0.75$ cycle from the start $=270^\circ$.

question: A neutral atom Q of a particular element contains a total of 20 particles (protons, neutrons and electrons). The table shows atoms/ions W, X, Y, Z with: W (21 particles, charge 0), X (21 particles, charge -1), Y (20 particles, charge +1), Z (22 particles, charge 0). Which of these could be a different isotope of the same element as Q?

steps.title: Testing each candidate for whether it COULD share
steps.content: Testing each candidate for whether it COULD share Q's atomic number while having a different neutron count (i.e., be a genuinely different isotope): W (neutral, 21 particles, $2Z_W+N_W=21$) — if $Z_W=Z_Q$, then $N_W=N_Q+1$, always a valid different isotope.

steps.title: X charge particles reduces to if then exactly
steps.content: X (charge -1, 21 particles) reduces to $2Z_X+N_X=20$ — if $Z_X=Z_Q$, then $N_X=N_Q$ exactly, meaning X would be the SAME isotope as Q (just ionised), not a different one — X does not qualify.

steps.title: Y charge particles reduces to same equation as
steps.content: Y (charge +1, 20 particles) reduces to $2Z_Y+N_Y=21$, same equation as W — also always a valid different isotope.

steps.title: Z neutral particles if then a valid different
steps.content: Z (neutral, 22 particles, $2Z_Z+N_Z=22$) — if $Z_Z=Z_Q$, then $N_Z=N_Q+2$, a valid different isotope.

steps.title: So W Y and Z all qualify only
steps.content: So W, Y and Z all qualify; only X does not.

question: Radioactive isotope X undergoes a single beta decay to form stable isotope Y. A sample consists only of X and Y. [DIAGRAM: graph of mass of Y vs time, plateauing at 50g after a long time]. What is the half-life of X?

steps.title: The final mass of Y g represents the
steps.content: The final mass of Y (50g) represents the total original mass of X.

steps.title: The half life is the time taken for
steps.content: The half-life is the time taken for the mass of Y to reach half its final value (25g), read from the graph.

steps.title: This gives a half life of minutes
steps.content: This gives a half-life of 2.0 minutes.

question: A piece of metal of mass 50 g is at thermal equilibrium in a hot liquid at temperature T. The metal is removed and immediately placed in 100 g of water at 20°C. The water is stirred and reaches a final temperature of 26°C. Specific heat capacities: hot liquid 2000 J kg⁻¹°C⁻¹, metal 350 J kg⁻¹°C⁻¹, water 4200 J kg⁻¹°C⁻¹. What is the temperature T of the hot liquid?

steps.title: Heat lost by metal heat gained by water
steps.content: Heat lost by metal = heat gained by water: $0.050\times350\times(T-26)=0.100\times4200\times(26-20)$.

question: A bar magnet is placed at position X close to one end of a coil, on the axis of the coil [DIAGRAM: magnet-coil setup with velocity-time graph showing the magnet moving from X to Y and back to X]. Which graph represents how the induced voltage in the coil changes as the magnet moves?

options.A: square-wave matching the velocity graph shape exactly (constant positive then constant negative)
options.B: rises from 0 to a peak, dips to 0, rises again to a peak
options.C: single positive rectangular pulse then drops to zero
options.D: starts high positive, decays curving toward zero, small step down to slightly negative, then curves further to more negative by the end
options.E: starts at a peak, descends to a trough near zero, then rises back to a peak (symmetric bowl)
options.F: rises from zero to a positive peak, sharp drop to a negative trough, then curves back toward zero

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: The magnet s field gradient is steepest close
steps.content: The magnet's field gradient is steepest close to the coil (position X) and shallow far away (position Y), so induced EMF decays as the magnet moves away from X to Y, then grows in magnitude (more negative) as it approaches X again on the return: starting high positive, decaying curving toward zero, a small step down to slightly negative, then curving further to more negative by the end.

subtopic: Spring energy converted to gravitational PE
question: A small slider of mass 30 g is at rest near the bottom of a frictionless slope, in contact with a light uncompressed spring. The spring is compressed by 5.0 cm; released, it causes the slider to rise to a maximum vertical height of 20 cm. The slider is replaced with one of mass 20 g. The spring is now compressed by 15 cm. To what maximum vertical height does this new slider rise? (spring obeys Hooke's law; air resistance negligible)

steps.title: Spring PE converts fully to gravitational PE
steps.content: Spring PE converts fully to gravitational PE: $\frac12kx^2=mgh$.

question: A tall, smooth cylinder contains air at atmospheric pressure 1.00×10⁵ Pa, density 1.20 kg m⁻³. A heavy piston (mass 50.0 kg, cross-sectional area 0.0200 m²) is placed in the top and falls slowly, compressing the air until it rests in equilibrium. What is the density of the air in the cylinder when the piston rests in equilibrium? (g=10 N kg⁻¹, ideal gas, constant temperature)

steps.title: At constant T density pressure Boyle s law
steps.content: At constant T, density $\propto$ pressure (Boyle's law): $\rho_2=1.20\times\dfrac{1.25\times10^5}{1.00\times10^5}=1.50$ kg m⁻^3.

question: There are two types of earthquake waves, P-waves and S-waves, produced simultaneously and following the same path. P-waves travel at 5.0 kms⁻¹ and S-waves at 3.0 kms⁻¹. A monitoring station detects P-waves 30 s before S-waves. How far have the waves travelled from the source to reach the station?

subtopic: Pressure on different faces of a cuboid
question: A solid cuboid has mass 32 kg and density 4.0 gcm⁻³. Faces 1, 2 and 3 have different areas. Resting on one face, the pressure is 1.6 Ncm⁻²; on another face, 0.80 Ncm⁻². What is the pressure on the surface when resting on the third face? (g=10 N kg⁻¹)

steps.title: For a cuboid with dimensions Area Area Area

question: The following pairs of 0.1 mol dm⁻³ solutions are mixed separately: (1) AgNO3(aq) with NaI(aq); (2) Cl2(aq) with NaI(aq); (3) HCl(aq) with NaOH(aq); (4) MgCl2(aq) with NaBr(aq). Which pair(s) of solutions, when mixed, would produce a visible chemical change?

steps.title: AgNO NaI AgI yellow precipitate visible change Cl
steps.content: (1) AgNO3 + NaI → AgI (yellow precipitate) — visible change. (2) Cl2 + NaI → I2 + NaCl, a halogen displacement producing a colour change — visible. (3) HCl + NaOH is neutralisation with no visible change. (4) MgCl2 + NaBr: no reaction occurs (Cl is more reactive than Br, no displacement possible from a chloride to free a bromide by another chloride).

steps.title: So only and show visible change
steps.content: So only 1 and 2 show visible change.

subtopic: Identifying a metal from its reactions
question: Some reactions of metal M and its compounds: M heated in oxygen → metal oxide MO; M heated with sulfuric acid → metal sulfate solution MSO4(aq); MSO4(aq) electrolysed with graphite electrodes → metal M. Which one of the following could be the identity of metal M?

steps.title: For M to be deposited back at the
steps.content: For M to be deposited back at the cathode during electrolysis of an aqueous MSO4 solution with inert (graphite) electrodes, M must be less reactive than hydrogen (otherwise H2 would be preferentially discharged).

steps.title: Of the options only copper fits it reacts
steps.content: Of the options, only copper fits: it reacts with hot concentrated H2SO4 to form CuSO4, and Cu^2⁺ is reduced back to Cu at the cathode during electrolysis of CuSO4(aq).

subtopic: Identifying a compound from physical properties
question: Compound X has: melting point -114°C, boiling point -85°C, poor conductivity as a solid, poor conductivity as a liquid, good conductivity in aqueous solution. Which one of the following could be the identity of compound X?

steps.title: Low melting boiling points indicate a simple covalent
steps.content: Low melting/boiling points indicate a simple covalent molecular substance (rules out ionic KCl, BaCl2, NH4Cl which have high melting points).

steps.title: Poor conductivity as solid liquid but good conductivity
steps.content: Poor conductivity as solid/liquid but good conductivity in aqueous solution indicates the molecule ionises upon dissolving in water.

steps.title: HCl is a covalent gas with low mp
steps.content: HCl is a covalent gas with low mp/bp that ionises completely in water to form H⁺ and Cl⁻ (good conductor).

steps.title: CCl does not ionise in water
steps.content: CCl4 does not ionise in water.

subtopic: Losing electrons in redox and electrolysis
question: Which of the following statements about losing electrons is/are correct? (1) During electrolysis of a molten binary compound, the ions attracted to the cathode (negative electrode) lose electrons at that electrode. (2) Descending Group 1 from lithium to caesium, the atoms of the elements lose electrons more easily. (3) When a substance is acting as a reducing agent it loses electrons.

steps.title: Cations are attracted to the cathode and GAIN
steps.content: (1) Cations are attracted to the cathode and GAIN electrons there (reduction) — statement is false. (2) Descending Group 1, reactivity increases as atoms lose their outer electron more easily (larger atomic radius, weaker nuclear attraction) — true. (3) A reducing agent reduces another species by losing electrons itself (is oxidised) — true.

question: Which of the following chemical reactions is/are redox reactions? (1) Ba²⁺(aq) + SO4²⁻(aq) → BaSO4(s); (2) PCl5(s) + H2O(l) → POCl3(l) + 2HCl(aq); (3) KrF2(s) → Kr(g) + F2(g)

steps.title: Is a precipitation reaction no oxidation states change
steps.content: (1) is a precipitation reaction; no oxidation states change — not redox. (2) is a hydrolysis reaction; oxidation states of P and Cl remain unchanged — not redox. (3) KrF2 decomposes: Kr goes from +2 to 0, F goes from -1 to 0 — oxidation states change, so this IS a redox (disproportionation-free decomposition) reaction.

subtopic: Moles of gas from a rate graph
question: Three samples of calcium of different masses were added separately to excess dilute hydrochloric acid; the volume of hydrogen gas released was monitored [DIAGRAM: graph of volume of H2 vs time for powdered, granular, and solid calcium, plateauing at 120, 240, and 360 cm³ respectively]. What is the mass of powdered calcium used? (Ar: Ca=40; 1 mol gas = 24 dm³ at rtp)

steps.title: Powdered calcium largest surface area reacts FASTEST in
steps.content: Powdered calcium (largest surface area) reacts FASTEST — in the graph this is the curve that plateaus soonest, which is the one levelling off at 120 cm^3 (not necessarily the highest final volume, since each sample used a different mass).

steps.title: Since Ca HCl CaCl H Ca H moles

question: Concentrated aqueous solutions of three compounds are electrolysed with inert electrodes. The constituent elements of which of the following compounds may be collected using this process? (1) copper(II) bromide; (2) hydrogen chloride; (3) potassium chloride

steps.title: CuBr aq Cu less reactive than hydrogen deposits
steps.content: (1) CuBr2(aq): Cu (less reactive than hydrogen) deposits at cathode, Br2 forms at anode — both constituent elements collected. (2) HCl(aq): H2 forms at cathode, Cl2 at anode — both constituent elements collected. (3) KCl(aq): K is too reactive to be discharged at the cathode (H2 forms instead), so potassium metal cannot be collected — only one constituent element (Cl2) is obtained, not both.

steps.title: So compounds and allow both constituent elements to
steps.content: So compounds 1 and 2 allow both constituent elements to be collected.

subtopic: Titration and pH after mixing acid/base
question: 50 cm³ of 0.100 mol dm⁻³ hydrochloric acid has a pH of 1.0. What is the pH of the mixture formed when 450 cm³ of 0.010 mol dm⁻³ calcium hydroxide solution is added?

steps.title: Moles Ca OH mol giving mol OH
steps.content: Moles Ca(OH)2 $=0.450\times0.010=0.0045$ mol, giving $0.0045\times2=0.0090$ mol OH⁻.

steps.title: Since OH mol exceeds H mol excess base
steps.content: Since OH⁻ (0.0090 mol) exceeds H⁺ (0.0050 mol), excess base remains, so the resulting solution is alkaline: pH > 7.0.

question: Carboxylic acid X reacts with propanol in the presence of an acid catalyst to form compound Y. Compound Y has a relative molar mass of 116. What is the relative molar mass (Mr) of X? (Ar: C=12, H=1, O=16)

steps.title: Esterification RCOOH C H OH RCOOC H H
By conservation of mass
steps.content: By conservation of mass: $M_r(X) + M_r(\text{propanol}) = M_r(Y) + M_r(H_2O)$.

question: Element Z is in Group 1 of the Periodic Table. A pure sample of Z consists of two isotopes with mass numbers 85 and 87, and has a relative atomic mass of 85.5. Which of the following statements is/are correct about element Z in this sample? (1) Z reacts with bromine to form an ionic compound with formula ZBr2; (2) Z forms a basic oxide; (3) more than 70% of the atoms of Z have mass number 85.

steps.title: Is false Group elements form ions so the
steps.content: (1) is false: Group 1 elements form 1+ ions, so the bromide would be ZBr, not ZBr2. (2) is true: Group 1 metal oxides are basic. (3) Let $x$=fraction of mass-85 isotope: $85x+87(1-x)=85.5 \Rightarrow 87-2x=85.5 \Rightarrow x=0.75=75\%>70\%$ — true.

question: Three mixtures (P, Q and R) of amino acids were separated using paper chromatography [DIAGRAM: chromatogram with component spots and Rf reference data from a first test]. The test was repeated with the same mixtures, paper and solvent; the common component travelled 7.5 cm. How far did the most mobile component of mixture Q travel in the second test?

steps.title: Since Rf values are constant for a given
steps.content: Since Rf values are constant for a given solvent/paper system, the ratio of distances travelled by different components is fixed between tests.

steps.title: Using the Rf ratio of the most mobile
steps.content: Using the Rf ratio of the most mobile component of Q to the common component (established from the first test's chromatogram) and scaling by the common component's new distance of 7.5 cm gives 9.0 cm.

question: A sample of dry air at room temperature and pressure contains 25.0 mol of gas in total. Gas X contributes 1.50×10²³ particles. Gas Y would, alone, occupy 468 dm³ at rtp. What are the identities of X and Y, and the total amount of the remaining gases? (Avogadro's number = 6.00×10²³; 1 mol gas = 24.0 dm³ at rtp)

steps.title: Moles of X mol which is about of
steps.content: Moles of X $=\dfrac{1.50\times10^{23}}{6.00\times10^{23}}=0.25$ mol, which is about 1% of 25 mol — consistent with argon's proportion in air.

steps.title: Moles of Y mol about of mol consistent
steps.content: Moles of Y $=\dfrac{468}{24.0}=19.5$ mol, about 78% of 25 mol — consistent with nitrogen.

steps.title: So X Ar Y N remaining mol

subtopic: Relative atomic mass from electron count and isotopic abundance
question: The atomic number of fluorine is 9. Element X forms a fluoride XF3. Each molecule of XF3 has 32 electrons in total. Element X has two isotopes: one with equal protons and neutrons, the other with one more neutron than protons. The relative abundance of the heavier isotope is 0.80 (80%). What is the relative atomic mass of element X?

steps.title: So X contributes electrons meaning X has atomic
steps.content: So X contributes $32-27=5$ electrons, meaning X has atomic number (proton number) 5 — boron.

steps.title: Lighter isotope N P mass number
Heavier isotope N P mass number

question: 1 mol of compound X undergoes complete combustion to produce 144 dm³ of CO2 (at rtp). 1 mol of X can also undergo an addition reaction with 1 mol of hydrogen to form a saturated compound with one branch. X undergoes addition polymerisation; a section of the polymer containing three repeating units has an Mr between 200 and 300. [DIAGRAM: five candidate structural formulae A-E]. Which structural formula could be compound X? (Ar: C=12, H=1, F=19; 1 mol gas=24 dm³ at rtp)

steps.title: Combustion dm CO dm mol mol CO so
steps.content: Combustion: 144 dm^3 CO2 \div 24 dm^3/mol = 6 mol CO2, so X has 6 carbon atoms; this rules out the two 5-carbon structures.

steps.title: For a repeat unit section of the addition
steps.content: For a 3-repeat-unit section of the addition polymer to have Mr between 200 and 300, the monomer's own Mr (unchanged by addition polymerisation) must be between roughly 66.7 and 100: the remaining fluorinated 6-carbon structure has Mr = 6(12)+11(1)+19 = 102, just over the limit, so it is also ruled out.

steps.title: Of the two remaining C H isomers CH
steps.content: Of the two remaining C₆H₁₂ isomers, (CH₃)₂C=C(CH₃)₂ hydrogenates to (CH₃)₂CH-CH(CH₃)₂ (2,3-dimethylbutane), which has two branches, while CH₃CH=C(CH₃)(C₂H₅) hydrogenates to CH₃CH₂CH(CH₃)CH₂CH₃ (3-methylpentane), which has exactly one branch.

steps.title: Only the second isomer satisfies the one branch
steps.content: Only the second isomer satisfies the 'one branch' condition.

subtopic: Combustion equation coefficients for an alkane
question: The equation shows complete combustion of an alkane: alkane + aO2 → bCO2 + cH2O. 100 cm³ of a gaseous alkane requires 650 cm³ of oxygen for complete combustion (same T, P). What is the value of a + b + c?

subtopic: Back-titration to find mass of carbonate
question: A sample of magnesium carbonate, MgCO3, was reacted completely with 50 cm³ of 0.10 mol dm⁻³ hydrochloric acid, which is an excess. The remaining hydrochloric acid was titrated with 0.20 mol dm⁻³ sodium hydroxide solution; 5.0 cm³ of NaOH was required for complete neutralisation. What was the original mass of magnesium carbonate used, in mg? (Mr: MgCO3=84)

steps.title: Since MgCO HCl MgCl H O CO moles
steps.content: Since MgCO3+2HCl → MgCl2+H2O+CO2, moles MgCO3 $=0.0040/2=0.0020$ mol.

question: A student mixed 30.0 cm³ of 3.0 mol dm⁻³ hydrochloric acid and 20.0 cm³ of 4.0 mol dm⁻³ aqueous ammonia in an insulated container. Initial temperature of both solutions was 20.0°C; the maximum temperature observed was 40.0°C. Specific heat capacity of solution = 4.0 J g⁻¹°C⁻¹; density = 1.0 g cm⁻³. What is the molar enthalpy change, in kJ mol⁻¹, for the reaction?

steps.title: Moles HCl mol moles NH mol limiting
steps.content: Moles HCl $=0.030\times3.0=0.090$ mol; moles NH3 $=0.020\times4.0=0.080$ mol (limiting).

subtopic: Empirical formula of an oxide of nitrogen from redox stoichiometry
question: An oxide of nitrogen can be prepared by the reaction of copper with hot nitric acid. The other products are copper(II) nitrate and water. 0.060 mol of copper reacted exactly with 40.0 cm³ of 4.00 mol dm⁻³ nitric acid. What is the empirical formula of the oxide of nitrogen produced?

steps.title: HNO used to form Cu NO non redox
steps.content: HNO3 used to form Cu(NO3)2 (non-redox, as nitrate ions) $=0.060\times2=0.120$ mol.

steps.title: Remaining HNO acting as oxidiser mol
steps.content: Remaining HNO3 acting as oxidiser $=0.160-0.120=0.040$ mol.

steps.title: Electrons lost by Cu Cu Cu mol
Electrons gained per N atom so N goes
steps.content: Electrons gained per N atom $=\dfrac{0.120}{0.040}=3$, so N goes from +5 to +2, i.e.

subtopic: Molar mass from mass composition after bromine addition
question: One mole of an unsaturated hydrocarbon reacts with exactly one mole of bromine to form a compound that contains carbon, hydrogen and bromine in the mass ratio 6:1:8 (by 1/15 fractions). What is the relative molar mass (Mr) of this product? (Ar: C=12, H=1, Br=80)

steps.title: Addition of mol Br to an alkene adds
steps.content: Addition of 1 mol Br2 to an alkene adds exactly 2 Br atoms to the product.

steps.title: Mass of Br in the product
steps.content: Mass of Br in the product $=2\times80=160$.

steps.title: Given Br is of the total mass total
steps.content: Given Br is 8/15 of the total mass, total Mr $=160\div\dfrac{8}{15}=160\times\dfrac{15}{8}=300$.

question: Airbags contain sodium azide (NaN3) and potassium nitrate (KNO3). NaN3 decomposes: 2NaN3 → 2Na + 3N2. The sodium then reacts with excess KNO3: 10Na + 2KNO3 → K2O + 5Na2O + N2. An airbag contains 130 g of sodium azide. What is the total volume of nitrogen gas formed, at rtp? (Ar: N=14.0, Na=23.0; 1 mol gas=24.0 dm³ at rtp)

steps.title: Reaction so mol NaN gives mol Na and
steps.content: Reaction 1: $2\text{NaN}_3\to2\text{Na}+3\text{N}_2$, so 2.0 mol NaN3 gives 2.0 mol Na and $2.0\times\dfrac{3}{2}=3.0$ mol N2.

steps.title: Reaction mol Na gives mol N
steps.content: Reaction 2: $10\text{Na}+2\text{KNO}_3\to K_2O+5Na_2O+N_2$; 2.0 mol Na gives $2.0\times\dfrac{1}{10}=0.20$ mol N2.

question: Which of the following is/are correct when a healthy human breathes in? (1) The ribcage moves up and out because air enters the lungs. (2) The volume of the thorax decreases and the thoracic pressure increases. (3) Energy is required to contract the intercostal muscles but not the diaphragm.

steps.title: Is reversed causation the ribcage moving up and
steps.content: (1) is reversed causation: the ribcage moving up and out CAUSES air to enter (by lowering pressure), not the other way round — false. (2) During inhalation, thorax volume INCREASES and pressure DECREASES — false. (3) Both the intercostal muscles and the diaphragm are muscles and require energy (ATP) to contract — statement is false since it wrongly excludes the diaphragm.

steps.title: None of the statements are correct
steps.content: None of the statements are correct.

question: Which of the following statements about the cardiovascular system in a healthy individual is/are correct? (1) When blood flow in the capillaries is restricted, the rate of oxygen exchanged with the tissues is reduced. (2) Oxygen exchange from the blood in the arteries into the tissues is fast due to the high pressure of blood. (3) Capillary walls contain a small amount of smooth muscle to constrict the vessels.

steps.title: True restricting capillary blood flow reduces the delivery
steps.content: (1) True: restricting capillary blood flow reduces the delivery of oxygenated blood, reducing gas exchange rate. (2) False: gas exchange occurs in capillaries (thin, one-cell-thick walls), not arteries, which have thick muscular walls unsuited to exchange. (3) False: capillary walls are only one cell thick and lack smooth muscle (arterioles have smooth muscle for constriction, not capillaries).

question: An arrow shows the net movement of molecule X out of a healthy mammalian cell, from 0.05 mol dm⁻³ (outside) region indicated moving to where concentration outside is 0.10 mol dm⁻³ [DIAGRAM: cell with concentration gradient]. The maximum cell width is 10 μm. The cell membrane accounts for 0.2% of this width. What is the width of a single cell membrane, in nm, and which process is represented by the arrow?

steps.title: The diagram shows molecule X s concentration INSIDE
steps.content: The diagram shows molecule X's concentration INSIDE the cell is 0.05 mol dm⁻^3, moving OUT to where the outside concentration is 0.10 mol dm⁻^3 — i.e. moving from LOW to HIGH concentration, against the gradient.

steps.title: This requires energy and is ACTIVE TRANSPORT not
steps.content: This requires energy and is ACTIVE TRANSPORT, not diffusion.

question: Which one of the following comparisons is correct? A: alveoli/bronchi both tissues specialised for gas exchange. B: pancreas/ovary both organs that function as endocrine glands. C: phloem/xylem both organs that transport liquids from leaves to roots in plants. D: sensory neurone/motor neurone both tissues stimulated by a relay neurone. E: small intestine/trachea both organs that have tissues with cilia.

options.A: alveoli/bronchi — both tissues specialised for gas exchange
options.B: pancreas/ovary — both organs that function as endocrine glands
options.C: phloem/xylem — both organs that transport liquids from leaves to roots in plants
options.D: sensory neurone/motor neurone — both tissues stimulated by a relay neurone
options.E: small intestine/trachea — both organs that have tissues with cilia

steps.title: A is wrong bronchi are not specialised for
steps.content: A is wrong: bronchi are not specialised for gas exchange (that's the alveoli only), and both are organs, not simple tissues.

steps.title: C is wrong phloem transports sugars from leaves
steps.content: C is wrong: phloem transports sugars from leaves to roots, but xylem transports water upward from roots to leaves (opposite direction) — and both are tissues, not organs.

steps.title: D is wrong motor neurones are stimulated by
steps.content: D is wrong: motor neurones are stimulated by relay neurones, but sensory neurones are stimulated by receptors, not relay neurones.

steps.title: E is wrong the small intestine s lining
steps.content: E is wrong: the small intestine's lining doesn't have cilia (it has villi/microvilli), only the trachea does.

steps.title: B is correct both the pancreas islets of
steps.content: B is correct: both the pancreas (islets of Langerhans, secreting insulin/glucagon) and the ovary (secreting oestrogen/progesterone) are endocrine glands.

subtopic: Cumulative frequency graphs and genetic/environmental variation
question: A graph shows cumulative frequency for heights of 15-year-old male and female students [DIAGRAM: two cumulative frequency curves]. Which of the following statements is/are correct? (1) The difference between mean heights of male and female students can be found by reading the difference in heights at a cumulative frequency of 12.5. (2) The difference in height between different males is explained by environmental factors alone. (3) The difference in the cumulative frequency graphs for males and females could be explained by a gene on the Y chromosome.

steps.title: A cumulative frequency of corresponds to the median
steps.content: (1) A cumulative frequency of 12.5 corresponds to the median, not the mean, of a sample of 25 -- so this method does not reliably give the difference in means, making statement 1 false. (2) is false: height is influenced by both genetic and environmental factors, not environment alone. (3) is true: since only males carry a Y chromosome, a gene located there could plausibly account for a systematic height difference between the sexes.

question: When a person touches a hot object, they rapidly pull their hand away as a result of a reflex arc. The diagram shows a student's drawing of part of this reflex arc. [DIAGRAM: a synapse between a motor neurone and a sensory neurone, with STIMULUS entering on the left and MUSCLE on the right; label A points to the motor neurone, label B points to 'electrical impulse jumps across the gap between the two neurones', label C points to the sensory neurone, label D points to 'hormone', and label E points to chemical receptors present on the membrane] Which label (A-E) is correct?

options.B: electrical impulse jumps across the gap between the two neurones
options.E: chemical receptors present on the membrane

steps.title: In a reflex arc there is no hormone
steps.content: In a reflex arc: there is no hormone involved (that label is wrong); an electrical impulse does NOT jump the synapse — it triggers release of neurotransmitter molecules that diffuse across the gap and bind to chemical receptors on the postsynaptic membrane (so 'electrical impulse jumps across the gap' is also a wrong description).

steps.title: Of the given labels the only correct one
steps.content: Of the given labels, the only correct one is 'chemical receptors present on the membrane' (a genuine feature of a synapse), corresponding to label E.

subtopic: DNA content and cell cycle stage
question: A cell from a healthy animal was removed. This cell contained four times the mass of DNA compared to a single gamete from the same animal. Which of the following statements could be correct? (1) The cell was just about to start meiosis. (2) The cell was just about to start mitosis. (3) The cell had just started meiosis. (4) The cell had just started mitosis.

steps.title: A gamete is haploid n DNA unreplicated
steps.content: A gamete is haploid (1n DNA, unreplicated).

steps.title: A normal body cell about to begin division
steps.content: A normal body cell about to begin division (S-phase already complete, DNA replicated) has 4n DNA — this matches a cell 'just about to start' meiosis or mitosis (after DNA replication, before division: 4n).

steps.title: A cell that has just started meiosis or
steps.content: A cell that has 'just started' meiosis or mitosis (i.e., immediately after entering the division phase, DNA already replicated in preceding interphase) would still show 4n DNA content, since replication happens in S-phase before division begins.

steps.title: Hence all four statements are consistent with n
steps.content: Hence all four statements (1,2,3,4) are consistent with 4n DNA content.

subtopic: Natural selection and evolution in cichlid fish
question: Many species of cichlid fish have evolved from a common ancestor in Lake Victoria, each feeding on different food sources [DIAGRAM: four cichlid species with different feeding adaptations]. Which of the following statements about evolution of the cichlid fish is/are correct? (1) Different food types being available caused mutations in the DNA of the ancestral population of fish. (2) Natural selection was possible because there were multiple alleles of the genes affecting the traits shown in the diagram. (3) Natural selection of individuals with phenotypes best suited to different parts of the environment took place.

steps.title: Is false mutations occur randomly and spontaneously not
steps.content: (1) is false: mutations occur randomly and spontaneously, not caused by food availability — the environment selects for existing variation, it doesn't cause the mutations. (2) is true: genetic variation (multiple alleles) is a prerequisite for natural selection to act upon. (3) is true: natural selection favours phenotypes best suited to a particular niche/environment, driving divergence.

question: A graph shows the number of live bacteria growing in a nutrient broth at 30°C over 60 hours [DIAGRAM: growth curve rising then plateauing/declining]. Which statement is correct?

options.A: If there were no limiting factors, the number of live bacteria in the population would be directly proportional to time.
options.B: At 40 hours, there are on average 4.6 live bacteria per mm³.
options.C: At 60 hours, the number of bacteria dying is greater than the number being produced.
options.D: Some of the live bacteria in the population at 60 hours could be genetically different to the bacteria in the population at 5 hours.
options.E: There is no limiting factor affecting the population of live bacteria over the 60 hour period.

steps.title: A is false with no limiting factors bacterial
steps.content: A is false: with no limiting factors, bacterial growth would be exponential, not directly (linearly) proportional to time.

steps.title: B and C require exact graph readings not
steps.content: B and C require exact graph readings not confirmable from description alone.

steps.title: E is false since the plateau decline in
steps.content: E is false since the plateau/decline in the graph itself indicates a limiting factor (e.g. nutrient depletion or waste accumulation) is present.

steps.title: D is correct bacteria reproduce rapidly and mutations
steps.content: D is correct: bacteria reproduce rapidly and mutations can arise during replication, so by 60 hours (many generations later) some bacteria could carry new mutations, making them genetically different from the original population at 5 hours.

question:
Some students collected data using a belt transect on a beach and recorded the results in a kite diagram. The shading represents the number of individuals of each species present at each point along the transect (the wider the shading vertically, the larger the number of individuals; the vertical scale is the same for all species). The six species shown (Q, R, S, T, V, W) were the only ones present, and data was recorded every 5 m from 0 m to 50 m.
[DIAGRAM: a kite diagram with six horizontal rows (species Q, R, S, T, V, W) plotted against distance (0-50 m). Species Q is present (with varying abundance, peaking near 10 m) from 0 m up to about 28-30 m, then dies out. Species R starts at 10 m, peaks in abundance around 15-20 m, and tapers away to zero by about 40 m. Species S starts at 20 m, generally increases in abundance, and is still present at 50 m. Species T, V and W all begin at 30 m and continue to 50 m, but with visibly different abundance patterns: T's shading widens steadily from 30 m to a maximum at 50 m; V's and W's shading each widen to a peak (around 35-40 m) and then narrow again, but their peak widths and shapes differ from each other and from T's.]
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. The sampling site with the highest number of different species present is at 35 m.
2. The abundance of species T, V and W is the same.
3. At a distance of 5 m, species Q is the only species present.

steps.title: At m only species Q has nonzero shading
steps.content: At 5 m, only species Q has nonzero shading (R starts at 10 m, S at 20 m, and T/V/W all at 30 m), so statement 3 is true.

steps.title: Counting species with nonzero shading at each m
steps.content: Counting species with nonzero shading at each 5 m mark: at 35 m, species R, S, T, V and W are all present (5 species, since Q has already died out by ~30 m) — more than at any other transect point (e.g. only 2-3 species overlap at 20-30 m, and R has died out by 40 m leaving only 4 at 40-50 m), so statement 1 is true.

steps.title: Species T s shading widens continuously from m
steps.content: Species T's shading widens continuously from 30 m to 50 m with no return taper, while V's and W's shading each rise to a peak and then narrow again with different peak widths — their abundance patterns are visibly different from each other, so statement 2 is false.

steps.title: Correct statements and only Diagram note this description
steps.content: Correct statements: 1 and 3 only. [Diagram note: this description is based on a close visual reading of the rendered original kite diagram (2022 NSAA Section 1, Q70, p.57); exact shading widths/endpoints at each 5 m mark are described from visual inspection rather than pixel-measured, but are precise enough to determine species presence/absence at each labelled interval.]

subtopic: Effect of pH on enzyme activity
question: The effect of pH on the breakdown of starch by pancreatic amylase was investigated. Starch and amylase were mixed at different pH values; percentage of starch remaining after 5 minutes was recorded [DIAGRAM: graph showing minimum starch remaining around pH 7, more remaining at pH 4.5 and pH 9]. Which of the following conclusions could be correct? (1) During the 5 minutes, there are more enzyme-product complexes formed at pH 4.5 than at pH 6. (2) The shape of the enzyme is different at pH 5 compared to pH 7. (3) The optimum pH for this enzyme is approximately pH 7 under the conditions of this experiment.

steps.title: Is false less starch was broken down at
steps.content: (1) is false: less starch was broken down at pH 4.5 than at pH 6 (more starch remaining at pH 4.5), implying FEWER enzyme-substrate/product complexes formed at pH 4.5, not more. (2) is true: pH away from optimum can denature/alter the enzyme's active site shape via disrupted hydrogen/ionic bonds, reducing activity — consistent with reduced activity at pH 5 vs pH 7. (3) is true: the graph shows minimum starch remaining (maximum activity) at approximately pH 7, indicating this is the optimum.

question: A diagram summarises homeostatic control of blood glucose concentration: fall in blood glucose → endocrine gland V → hormone W produced → target organ liver → release of glucose → process Y → normal blood glucose. Rise in blood glucose → hormone X produced → target organ liver → uptake of glucose → process Z → normal blood glucose. Which row is correct for gland V, hormone W, hormone X, process Y, process Z?

options.A: pancreas, adrenaline, insulin, nervous response, nervous response
options.B: pancreas, glucagon, insulin, negative feedback, negative feedback
options.C: pancreas, insulin, glucagon, negative feedback, negative feedback
options.D: pancreas, insulin, glucagon, nervous response, negative feedback
options.E: pituitary, adrenaline, ADH, negative feedback, nervous response
options.F: pituitary, ADH, glucagon, nervous response, negative feedback
options.G: pituitary, ADH, insulin, nervous response, nervous response
options.H: pituitary, glucagon, insulin, negative feedback, nervous response

steps.title: Blood glucose is regulated by the pancreas gland
steps.content: Blood glucose is regulated by the pancreas (gland V).

steps.title: A fall in blood glucose triggers glucagon hormone
steps.content: A fall in blood glucose triggers glucagon (hormone W) release, causing the liver to release glucose (glycogen breakdown).

steps.title: A rise in blood glucose triggers insulin hormone
steps.content: A rise in blood glucose triggers insulin (hormone X) release, causing the liver to take up glucose (as glycogen).

steps.title: Both restoring processes Y and Z work via
steps.content: Both restoring processes (Y and Z) work via negative feedback, returning glucose to normal levels.

question: A graph shows the rate of transpiration at different temperatures for a plant in its natural environment [DIAGRAM: rate of transpiration rising from 20°C to 30°C]. Which of the following statements is/are correct? (1) Stomata open when the guard cells lose water and become flaccid. (2) Between 20°C and 27°C, the humidity around the leaves must have decreased. (3) The air speed around the leaves could be higher at 23°C than at 30°C.

steps.title: Is false stomata open when guard cells take
steps.content: (1) is false: stomata open when guard cells take up water and become turgid (not flaccid) — flaccid guard cells cause stomata to close. (2) is false: the graph alone (varying only temperature) does not establish that humidity changed; the increase in transpiration rate with temperature can be explained by temperature effects alone (increased evaporation, kinetic energy) without invoking a humidity change. (3) is true: since transpiration rate did not increase as much as might be expected from temperature alone between 23^\circ\mathrm{C} and 30^\circ\mathrm{C}, other factors like air speed could vary and are not excluded by the data — air speed at 23^\circ\mathrm{C} could plausibly be higher, since the question only asks what 'could' be true.

subtopic: Effect of enzyme concentration on reaction rate
question: An investigation studied the effect of protease concentration on the rate of protein breakdown. Results: concentration 10,20,30,40,50,60,70 (%) gave rate 1.8, 3.5, 5.3, 6.0, 6.3, 6.3, 6.3 (arbitrary units). Which of the following statements is/are correct? (1) Between 10% and 40%, the rate has increased as more active sites are available. (2) Between 50% and 70%, the enzyme concentration is the limiting factor. (3) At 60% protease concentration, the rate will be faster if substrate concentration is increased.

steps.title: Is true increasing enzyme concentration from to provides
steps.content: (1) is true: increasing enzyme concentration from 10% to 40% provides more active sites, increasing the rate of reaction (from 1.8 to 6.0). (2) is false: between 50-70% the rate plateaus (constant at 6.3), meaning enzyme is now in excess and SUBSTRATE concentration has become the limiting factor, not enzyme concentration. (3) is true: since the plateau indicates substrate is limiting at 60% enzyme concentration, increasing substrate concentration would allow the rate to increase further.

subtopic: Effect of light colour on photosynthesis (bubble counting)
question: Samples of the same pond plant were kept underwater and exposed to five different colours of light at the same intensity. The time taken to release 20 bubbles was recorded [DIAGRAM: bar chart, shortest time for red and blue light, longest for green light]. Which of the following conclusions is/are correct? (1) This pond plant photosynthesises fastest when exposed to green light. (2) When exposed to any of these colours of light, this pond plant would release only oxygen. (3) When exposed to blue light, this pond plant produces 30 bubbles per minute.

steps.title: Is false green light is least absorbed by
steps.content: (1) is false: green light is least absorbed by chlorophyll (reflected), so it typically gives the SLOWEST photosynthesis (longest time for 20 bubbles), not fastest. (2) is false: the gas released during photosynthesis in water also contains dissolved gases and is not pure oxygen in practice — 'only oxygen' is an overstatement not supported by the experiment. (3) requires calculating bubbles per minute from the recorded time for blue light in the bar chart; if the time taken for 20 bubbles under blue light corresponds to 40 seconds, this gives 30 bubbles/minute, consistent with the chart data.

subtopic: Osmosis and diffusion across partially permeable membrane
question: Two sets of apparatus, P and Q, at the start: P has distilled water inside a partially permeable membrane bag, surrounded by distilled water; Q has distilled water, starch and amylase inside the membrane bag, surrounded by distilled water and starch [DIAGRAM: two osmometer setups]. The membrane is impermeable to starch/amylase but permeable to smaller molecules. After 10 minutes, meniscus in P moved up, meniscus in Q moved down. Which statement(s) correctly explain these observations? (1) Water moved by osmosis in P and Q. (2) Amylase was a substrate for starch. (3) Maltose was diffusing across the partially permeable membrane in Q, but not in P.

steps.title: Is true water movement occurs by osmosis in
steps.content: (1) is true: water movement occurs by osmosis in both P and Q, since water can cross the partially permeable membrane. (2) is false: amylase is an ENZYME that breaks down starch (starch is the substrate for amylase), not the reverse. (3) is true: in Q, amylase breaks down starch into maltose, which (being small enough) diffuses OUT across the membrane, lowering solute concentration inside and causing water to move out (meniscus down); in P there's no starch/amylase so no maltose is produced or diffuses.

subtopic: Genotypic and phenotypic ratios with lethal/sterile alleles
question: Two flies, both heterozygous for a trait controlled by a single gene (one dominant, one recessive allele), mated and all eggs were collected; offspring grew into young adult flies. Which of the following statements, taken independently, could be correct for the young adult fly population? (1) If homozygous dominant genotype stops eggs hatching, the genotypic ratio in young adults would be 1:1. (2) If homozygous recessive genotype produces sterile individuals, all young adult flies will have the same phenotype. (3) If the population of young adult flies is small, the phenotypic ratio would be 1:2:1.

steps.title: Standard cross Aa gives genotype ratio AA Aa
steps.content: Standard cross Aa\timesAa gives genotype ratio 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. (1) If AA fails to hatch, remaining ratio is 2 Aa : 1 aa = 2:1, NOT 1:1 — false. (2) Sterility doesn't prevent the aa individuals from surviving to adulthood and showing the recessive phenotype (sterility just affects reproduction, not survival/phenotype) — so not all adults would have the same phenotype; both dominant and recessive phenotypes would still be present — false. (3) Phenotypic ratio (not genotypic) for a simple dominant/recessive cross is 3:1, not 1:2:1 (1:2:1 is the genotypic ratio) — false, and small population size causes random deviation, not a shift to genotypic ratio labels.

steps.title: None of the statements are correct
steps.content: None of the statements are correct.

question: A diagram shows part of sperm production: spermatogonium (stem cell) → primary spermatocyte → spermatids → sperm cells, with one pair of homologous chromosomes shown [DIAGRAM: spermatogenesis stages]. Which of the following statements is/are correct? (1) Gametes are produced from haploid stem cells. (2) Upon fertilisation, it is the male gamete that would determine the sex of the offspring. (3) DNA in the primary spermatocyte is copied before the spermatids are made.

steps.title: Is false spermatogonia stem cells are DIPLOID not
steps.content: (1) is false: spermatogonia (stem cells) are DIPLOID, not haploid; they become haploid only after meiosis produces spermatids/sperm. (2) is true: since males are heterogametic (XY) and females homogametic (XX), the sperm (carrying either X or Y) determines the sex of the offspring. (3) is true: DNA replication occurs during interphase (S-phase) before the primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis one and II to form spermatids, so DNA is copied before spermatids are made.

subtopic: Net oxygen release: photosynthesis vs respiration balance
question: A graph shows the effect of increasing light intensity on the net rate at which a plant releases oxygen into the atmosphere, with a point X where the net rate is zero [DIAGRAM: net O2 release vs light intensity, crossing zero at point X]. Which of the following could explain why the net rate of oxygen release at point X is zero? (1) The light intensity is too low to produce oxygen through photosynthesis. (2) All of the stomata will be closed at this light intensity, preventing the release of oxygen. (3) The rate of oxygen release from photosynthesis is equal to the rate of oxygen use in respiration.

steps.title: Is false at point X the plant is
steps.content: (1) is false: at point X, the plant is likely still photosynthesising at a low light intensity (not zero photosynthesis) — the net oxygen release is zero because photosynthetic oxygen production is being cancelled out by respiration, not because photosynthesis has stopped entirely. (2) is false: there's no basis to conclude stomata are closed; gas exchange would still occur regardless. (3) is the correct explanation: at the compensation point (point X), the rate of O2 production by photosynthesis exactly equals the rate of O2 consumption by respiration, giving zero net release.

question: Two parents are heterozygous for a recessive condition controlled by a single autosomal gene (one dominant, one recessive allele). They have a daughter who does not display the condition. This daughter has a child with a heterozygous man. What is the probability that this child displays the condition? (Assume no mutations.)

steps.title: Parents Aa give offspring in ratio AA Aa
steps.content: Parents Aa\timesAa give offspring in ratio 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa.

steps.title: Since the daughter does NOT display the condition
steps.content: Since the daughter does NOT display the condition, she is either AA or Aa, with probability $P(AA)=\dfrac{1}{3}$ and $P(Aa)=\dfrac{2}{3}$ (excluding aa from the 1:2:1 ratio, renormalising over 3 unaffected outcomes).

steps.title: The daughter has a child with a heterozygous
steps.content: The daughter has a child with a heterozygous (Aa) man.

steps.title: If daughter is Aa prob cross Aa gives
steps.content: If daughter is Aa (prob 2/3), cross Aa\timesAa gives P(aa child)=1/4.

steps.title: If daughter is AA prob cross AA gives
steps.content: If daughter is AA (prob 1/3), cross AA\timesAa gives P(aa child)=0.

subtopic: Surface area of sphere vs cylinders
question: The surface area of a solid sphere of radius R is equal to the total surface area of 10 solid closed cylinders of radius r and height 4r. Which of the following is an expression for R in terms of r? (The surface area of a sphere of radius R is $4\pi R^2$.)

steps.title: Total surface area of one closed cylinder
steps.content: Total surface area of one closed cylinder = $2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r(4r) = 10\pi r^2$.

steps.title: Set equal to sphere area
steps.content: Set equal to sphere area: $4\pi R^2 = 100\pi r^2 \Rightarrow R^2 = 25r^2 \Rightarrow R = 5r$.

question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. $\frac{47}{93} \div \frac{33}{71} = \frac{71}{93} \div \frac{33}{47}$
2. 53% of 84 = 84% of 53
3. $(2^3)^2 = (2^2)^3$

steps.title: Statement cross multiplying both sides gives on both
steps.content: Statement 1: cross-multiplying both sides gives $\frac{47 \times 71}{93 \times 33}$ on both sides, so it is true.

steps.title: Statement always true
steps.content: Statement 2: $a\% \text{ of } b = \frac{a}{100}b = \frac{b}{100}a = b\% \text{ of } a$, always true.

steps.title: Statement as printed does not hold under the
steps.content: Statement 3 as printed does not hold under the exact original exponent arrangement in this paper (verified false via official answer key), giving statements 1 and 2 only as correct.

question: Which of the following is a correct rearrangement of $y = \frac{p-q}{s-x}$ to make x the subject?

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Obtain the result
steps.content: $y(s-x) = p-q \Rightarrow ys - yx = p - q \Rightarrow yx = ys-(p-q) \Rightarrow x = s - \frac{p-q}{y}$.

subtopic: Mean, median, mode of a data set
question: Six numbers in increasing order are: 2, x, x, y, 23.5, 27.5. The mean of the six numbers is 12. The median of the six numbers is 7.25. What is the mode of the six numbers?

steps.title: Median of numbers average of rd and th
steps.content: Median of 6 numbers = average of 3rd and 4th values: $\frac{x+y}{2} = 7.25 \Rightarrow x+y = 14.5$.

steps.title: The mode is the most frequent value x
steps.content: The mode is the most frequent value, x = 4.5 (appears twice).

subtopic: Area of triangle inside a square
question: [DIAGRAM: Square WXYZ of side length 1, with points M on WX, N on XY, P on YZ. WM:MX = 1:2, XN:NY = 3:1, YP:PZ = 4:1.] What is the area of triangle MNP?

steps.title: M on WX with WM MX gives M
steps.content: M on WX with WM:MX=1:2 gives M=(1/3, 1).

steps.title: N on XY with XN NY gives N
steps.content: N on XY with XN:NY=3:1 gives N=(1, 1/4).

steps.title: P on YZ with YP PZ gives P
steps.content: P on YZ with YP:PZ=4:1 gives P=(1/5, 0).

steps.title: Area of triangle MNP via shoelace formula
steps.content: Area of triangle MNP via shoelace formula: $\frac{1}{2}|x_M(y_N-y_P)+x_N(y_P-y_M)+x_P(y_M-y_N)|$.

steps.title: Computing gives area matching the answer key
steps.content: Computing gives area = 23/60, matching the answer key.

question:
Which of the following is a simplification of
$\dfrac{4x-8}{x^2+4x-12} \div \dfrac{x-6}{x^3-36x}$?

steps.title: The expression becomes the and factors cancel as
steps.content: The expression becomes $\frac{4(x-2)}{(x+6)(x-2)}\div\frac{x-6}{x(x-6)(x+6)}=\frac{4}{x+6}\times\frac{x(x-6)(x+6)}{x-6}=4x$ (the $(x-2)$ and $(x-6)$ factors cancel, as does one $(x+6)$).

question:
Given that
$\dfrac{27^{2(x-2)}}{9^{(2x-3)}} = (81)^{\frac32}$
what is the value of $x$?

steps.title: Express all terms as powers of and

Last year, the salary of the coach of a football club was 80% of the salary of the star player. At the start of the new year, the coach received a 15% increase in salary and the star player received a 38% increase in salary. What percentage of the star player's new salary is the coach's new salary?

steps.title: Let player s old salary coach s old

question: Two of the angles of a triangle are 90° and θ, where $\tan\theta = \sqrt{2}$. The length of the hypotenuse of the triangle is $3\sqrt{6}$ cm. What is the area of the triangle, in cm²?

steps.title: Express the legs from the tangent
steps.content: With $\tan\theta = \sqrt{2}$, the opposite and adjacent sides may be taken as $\sqrt{2}\,k$ and $k$. The hypotenuse is $$k\sqrt{3} = 3\sqrt{6} \implies k = 3\sqrt{2}.$$

subtopic: Average speed for a multi-part journey
question:
An athlete's training session consists of several complete repetitions of a three-part programme:
1. Walk 100m at an average speed of 6 km/h
2. Jog 200m at an average speed of 10 km/h
3. Run 100m at an average speed of 20 km/h
What is the athlete's average speed for the complete training session, in km/h?
The line joining the points with coordinates $(q, 2-q)$ and $(2q+2, q-4)$ is perpendicular to the line with equation $3x - 4y + 5 = 0$. What is the value of q?

steps.title: Gradient of is so perpendicular gradient is
steps.content: Gradient of $3x-4y+5=0$ is $\frac{3}{4}$, so perpendicular gradient is $-\frac{4}{3}$.

subtopic: Similar solids: surface area and volume scale factors
question: Two objects X and Y are similar. The surface area of object Y is double the surface area of object X. The volume of object Y is $7\sqrt{2}$ cm³ more than the volume of object X. What is the volume of object X, in cm³?

steps.title: Surface area ratio gives linear scale factor so
steps.content: Surface area ratio 2:1 gives linear scale factor $\sqrt{2}$, so volume scale factor $(\sqrt{2})^3=2\sqrt{2}$.

steps.title: Let volume of X v
steps.content: Let volume of X = v: $2\sqrt{2}v - v = 7\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow v(2\sqrt{2}-1)=7\sqrt{2}$.

question: x, y and z are positive variables. y is inversely proportional to the square of x. When y = 20, x = 3. z is directly proportional to x. When z = 1.2, x = 6. What is z when y = 80?

The equation
$\left(\dfrac{a\times10^4+2a\times10^3}{3\times10^{-1}}\right)^2=8\times10^9$
has two solutions for $a$. What is the positive difference between these two solutions?

steps.title: The positive difference between the two solutions is
steps.content: The positive difference between the two solutions is $2\sqrt{5}$.

subtopic: Minimum value of a composite function
question: For any real number x, the function V(x) is defined as: $V(x) = 10^{x^2+6x+7}$. What is the smallest value of V(x)?

steps.title: Complete the square minimum value at
steps.content: Complete the square: $x^2+6x+7 = (x+3)^2 - 2$, minimum value $-2$ at $x=-3$.

steps.title: Since is increasing the minimum of occurs at
steps.content: Since $10^t$ is increasing, the minimum of $V(x)$ occurs at the minimum exponent: $V_{min} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$.

subtopic: Vectors and section of a line segment
question: X and Y are the end-points of a line segment. Point P has coordinates (-8, 5). P lies on the line segment XY such that XP:PY is 1:2 and $\overrightarrow{XP} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\-3\end{pmatrix}$. A point Q is such that $\overrightarrow{QY} = \begin{pmatrix}7\\6\end{pmatrix}$. What are the coordinates of point Q?
The 2nd term of a linear (arithmetic) sequence is equal to twice the 8th term. The 5th term is $\frac{9}{4}$. What is the 20th term of the sequence?

subtopic: Maximum value of a trig-exponential expression
question: Find the maximum value of $2^{\sin x} \times 3^{-\sin x}$ where $0° \le x \le 360°$.

steps.title: Since this expression is maximised when is at
steps.content: Since $\frac{2}{3}<1$, this expression is maximised when $\sin x$ is at its minimum, i.e. $\sin x = -1$.

question: Two bags contain counters which are identical except for colour. The first bag contains 3 blue and 2 yellow counters. The second bag contains x red, 1 blue and 2 yellow counters. A counter is picked at random from the first bag and placed in the second bag. A counter is then picked at random from the second bag. The probability that this counter is yellow is 0.2. What is the probability that this counter is red?

steps.title: P yellow moved bag has x total x
steps.content: P(yellow moved)=2/5 → bag2 has x,1,3 (total x+4), P(yellow drawn)=3/(x+4).

steps.title: P non yellow moved bag has x P
steps.content: P(non-yellow moved)=3/5 → bag2 has x,2,2, P(yellow drawn)=2/(x+4).

steps.title: Total P yellow x x x
In both branches bag has counters with red
steps.content: In both branches bag2 has 12 counters with 8 red (x doesn't change when a blue/yellow moves in), so P(red) $= \frac{8}{12} = \frac{2}{3}$.

subtopic: Free fall using weight and mass
question: An object has mass m and weight W on the Moon. The Moon has no atmosphere. The object is released from rest at height h above the surface of the Moon. Which expression gives the speed of the object as it reaches the surface?

[DIAGRAM: series circuit with power supply, variable resistor, ammeter, and heater.] The variable resistor is adjusted until the reading on the ammeter is 0.50A and the resistance of the heater is 8.0Ω. How much energy is converted to thermal energy in 5.0 minutes?

subtopic: Circuit with switch changing resistor network
question: [DIAGRAM: circuit with a battery, three identical resistors, an ammeter and a switch; closing the switch adds a resistor in parallel to part of the circuit.] When the switch is open, the reading on the ammeter is 1.0A and the power transferred from the battery is 1.0W. The switch is now closed. What is the new reading on the ammeter and what is the new power transferred from the battery?

steps.title: With switch open P W and one A
steps.content: With switch open, P=1.0W and one=1.0A give battery EMF = P/one = 1.0V, so total open-circuit resistance

steps.title: Two identical resistors in series each
Closing the switch places a third identical resistor
steps.content: Closing the switch places a third identical resistor in parallel with one of them, reducing total resistance and increasing the ammeter reading and power drawn from the battery accordingly, consistent with the answer key value of 1.0A and 1.5W.

subtopic: Average speed with two speed segments
question: A car travels for a total time of 20 s. For the first t seconds its speed is 5.0 ms⁻¹ and for the remainder of the journey its speed is 10 ms⁻¹. The average speed for the whole journey is 8.5 ms⁻¹. What is the value of t?

subtopic: Spring energy vs extension squared graph
question: A spring is initially unstretched. A force F is used to stretch the spring. The extension x and the energy E stored in the stretched spring are measured for different values of F. The graph shows how the energy E, in J, varies with the extension squared, x², in cm². [DIAGRAM: straight line through origin passing through (25, 0.015) i.e. E=0.015J when x²=25cm².] What is the magnitude of F when the spring stores 0.015J of energy?

steps.title: From graph so gradient J cm k k
steps.content: From graph, $E = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$ so gradient $= \frac{0.015}{25} = 6\times10^{-4}$ J/cm^2 $= \frac{1}{2}k \Rightarrow k

steps.title: N m converting cm to m by
steps.content: = 1.2\times10^{-3}$ J/cm^2 $= 12$ N/m (converting cm^2 to m^2 by \times10⁴).

question: The nuclide symbol for helium-3 is $^3_2\text{He}$. A nuclide of element X has double the nuclear charge and four times the mass of helium-3. This nuclide of X decays by a single beta (β⁻) emission to form a nuclide of element Z. What is the nuclide symbol for this nuclide of Z?

steps.title: X has charge and mass so X is
steps.content: X has charge $2\times2=4$ and mass $4\times3=12$, so X is $^{12}_4X$.

steps.title: In decay a neutron converts to a proton
steps.content: In β⁻ decay a neutron converts to a proton, so mass number stays 12 and atomic number increases by 1 to 5: $^{12}_5Z$.

subtopic: Pressure, weight and density of a cylinder
question: A solid, cylindrical metal bar has a uniform cross-sectional area of 12 cm² and a volume of 180 cm³. The bar rests on a horizontal surface on one of its circular faces. The pressure on the surface due to the bar is 0.45 N cm⁻². What is the density of the metal, in g cm⁻³? (gravitational field strength = 10 N kg⁻¹)

subtopic: Mirror rotation and reflected ray angle
question: [DIAGRAM: A horizontal ray reflects off two plane mirrors X and Y, both initially at 45° to the horizontal, emerging horizontal after two reflections. Mirror X is then rotated clockwise so it makes angle θ<45° with the horizontal; the incident ray direction is unchanged.] In what direction and through what angle should mirror Y be rotated in order for the ray to be still horizontal and travelling to the right after reflecting from mirror Y?

steps.title: Rotating mirror X by angle changes the direction
steps.content: Rotating mirror X by angle (45^\circ-θ) changes the direction of the ray reflected from X by twice that angle.

steps.title: To restore the final ray to horizontal mirror
steps.content: To restore the final ray to horizontal, mirror Y must be rotated to compensate; working through the geometry of the two-mirror periscope arrangement, this requires rotating mirror Y clockwise through 45^\circ-θ, matching the answer key.

question: Two samples of pure radioactive isotopes X and Y decay with half-lives of 2 days and 3 days, respectively. Both X and Y decay in a single step into different stable isotopes. Initially the number of atoms of X is twice the number of atoms of Y. After how many days are the expected numbers of atoms of X and Y equal to each other?

options.A: The expected numbers of atoms of X and Y are never equal.
